roots.title: Visualization Theory, Homework 3, and Techniques
roots.summary: The lecture discusses visualization theory, emphasizing the evaluation and adaptation of visual systems. Homework 3 involves sentiment analysis with a $50,000 budget, expected to take 10-12 days, and students should manage resources carefully. Key topics include data transformations for enhancing visualizations, scatter plots for modeling relationships, the importance of correlation versus causation, and effective data visualization techniques using Matplotlib and Seaborn, addressing issues like overplotting and variable encoding.

children.title: Visualization Theory and Homework 3 Overview
children.summary: The lecture covers visualization theory, focusing on the evaluation and adaptation of visual systems. Homework 3 requires sentiment analysis using language models, backed by a $50,000 investment, and is expected to take 10 to 12 days. Students are advised to manage resources by running one tab at a time and minimizing kernel restarts to avoid budget issues and crashes.

children.title: Visualization Theory and Homework 3 Overview
children.summary: The lecture discusses visualization theory, emphasizing the evaluation and adaptation of visual systems. Homework 3 involves sentiment analysis in texts using language models, supported by a $50,000 investment, and is expected to take 10 to 12 days. Students should manage resources effectively by running one tab at a time and minimizing kernel restarts to prevent budget issues and crashes.

children.title: Visualization Theory and Homework 3 Overview
children.summary: The lecture covers visualization theory, focusing on evaluating the visual system's effectiveness and adapting visualizations. Homework 3 requires analyzing sentiment in texts using language models, funded by a $50,000 investment, and will take about 10 to 12 days to complete. Students are advised to manage resources carefully, running only one tab at a time and minimizing kernel restarts to avoid budget constraints and crashes.

children.title: Visualization Theory and Homework 3 Overview
children.summary: The lecture focuses on visualization theory, emphasizing how to evaluate the visual system's effectiveness and adapt visualizations accordingly. Homework 3 involves working with language models to analyze sentiment in various texts, funded by a $50,000 departmental investment, and will span approximately 10 to 12 days from its release.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: All right. Good morning, data 100. Welcome to our lecture eight. Please log into a slide there. There's going to be a number of fun polls today because it's all going to be about visualization theory to make sense of like how can we evaluate how good or sensitive our visual system is and then how to adapt the visualizations that we're creating based on that for exception. I start jumping into the content of today's lecture, I want to make some reminders here. Most importantly, the reminder that I have today is about homework tree. This is a very fun homework and also a very expensive homework for us. So our department kind of spent $50,000 on this homework so that you can work on this homework for 10 days or 12 days, kind of generally from the time that we released it from the time that we have the latest extension on the homework. So it's gonna be very fun. You're gonna be playing with... Some language models in order to get the sentiment of different different sentences and different articles. So it's going to be really fun. We wanted to give you this experience of working with different language models to make sense of how they work and how they are actually doing the embeddings or evaluations based on the small tweaks and changes that you're making in the sentences. I start jumping into the content of today's lecture, I want to make some reminders here. Most importantly, the reminder that I have today is about homework tree. This is a very fun homework and also a very expensive homework for us. So our department kind of spent $50,000 on this homework so that you can work on this homework for 10 days or 12 days, kind of generally from the time that we released it from the time that we have the latest extension on the homework. So it's gonna be very fun. You're gonna be playing with... Some language models in order to get the sentiment of different different sentences and different articles. So it's going to be really fun. children.title: Homework resource management and precautions
children.summary: Students are advised to be cautious while working on homework due to limited resources. It's important to run only one tab to avoid overloading the system and to wait for each cell to complete before running another. Restarting the kernel should be minimized, as it consumes resources and could lead to budget constraints.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So because this homework is very expensive, especially because at the time that you're looting the model and you're doing the inference on the model, there are some considerations that you have to have so that we can keep data hub active because last semester, the data hub basically crashed the data hub for almost a day, a pencil deploying that we could bump up the resources and then restart the homework. So that doesn't happen. One, all of you to think about all of the precautions that we have here. Know that each one of the cells that you're running, they might take a long time for those cells to run. Don't rerun stuff. Just wait patiently until you get the output of each one of the cells. So don't rerun stuff. Also, the budget that we have is going to be depending on each number of windows, perhaps, that are open on your laptop, right? So if you want to work on the homework, only open it in one tap. don't open it in multiple taps, because the number of taps that you're creating, it's like as if we have more students working on that homework and each one of those taps will be paying. And then it's not that the pay is that important, it's also important that our budget is only for 1200 students. So if all of you opens up a second tab, it's as if that we have 2400 students and we don't have budget for it, right? Budget in terms of resource allocation and the date and then we might crash later how they get. So don't reopen, wait for each one of the cells to run as usually it will take a long time. And then also a lot of time, we also wanna make sure that you don't restart the kernel and run everything over and over again. Only restart kernel, only if you need it because every time that you run everything, end to end, it will cost us. It will allocate resources to you and then it will cost us. Question in the box. This is just, I mean, generally the amount of resources that you're going to allocate, that you're going to basically use is depending on the number of tabs that you have open. But because all of the other homeworks are cheaper in a sense and they don't need resources that much, so it's not going to be that much of a concern for us. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: So because this homework is very expensive, especially because at the time that you're looting the model and you're doing the inference on the model, there are some considerations that you have to have so that we can keep data hub active because last semester, the data hub basically crashed the data hub for almost a day, a pencil deploying that we could bump up the resources and then restart the homework. So that doesn't happen. One, all of you to think about all of the precautions that we have here. Know that each one of the cells that you're running, they might take a long time for those cells to run. Don't rerun stuff. Just wait patiently until you get the output of each one of the cells. So don't rerun stuff. Also, the budget that we have is going to be depending on each number of windows, perhaps, that are open on your laptop, right? So if you want to work on the homework, only open it in one tap. don't open it in multiple taps, because the number of taps that you're creating, it's like as if we have more students working on that homework and each one of those taps will be paying. And then it's not that the pay is that important, it's also important that our budget is only for 1200 students. So if all of you opens up a second tab, it's as if that we have 2400 students and we don't have budget for it, right? Budget in terms of resource allocation and the date and then we might crash later how they get. So don't reopen, wait for each one of the cells to run as usually it will take a long time. And then also a lot of time, we also wanna make sure that you don't restart the kernel and run everything over and over again. Only restart kernel, only if you need it because every time that you run everything, end to end, it will cost us. It will allocate resources to you and then it will cost us. Question in the box. This is just, I mean, generally the amount of resources that you're going to allocate, that you're going to basically use is depending on the number of tabs that you have open. But because all of the other homeworks are cheaper in a sense and they don't need resources that much, so it's not going to be that much of a concern for us.

children.title: Important Note on Homework 03 Costs
children.summary: Homework 03 involves hosting a language model, which makes it the most expensive assignment in the course. Students should be mindful of resource usage on DataHub, as certain cells may take a long time to run. It is advised to avoid restarting the kernel or running multiple tabs to prevent crashes.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's just going to be a couple of bucks more per student. But this one is going to be very expensive if you all start doing that. So because of that, for this is specific homework, because it has a language model that you're hosting and you're hosting actually local in your instance, or your instance in the hub, we want to be mindful of the resources. Good question. But other homeworks are not as expensive. This is the most expensive homework that probably you will see in your entire data science kind of course work here at Berkeley. Video: 1: v

children.title: Introduction to Visualization and Transformations
children.summary: The focus is on building and visualizing relationships between variables through data transformations to enhance datasets and visualizations. Key topics include visualization theory, information channels in plots, and the importance of data transformation for modeling. Future discussions will cover probabilities, distributions, and linear regression models, along with a review of plotting distributions using histograms in Seaborn, KDE, and ECDF.

children.title: Introduction to Visualization and Transformations
children.summary: The lecture emphasizes building and visualizing relationships between variables, focusing on data transformations for solid datasets and effective visualizations. It covers visualization theory, the use of information channels in plots, and the significance of data transformation for modeling. Upcoming topics include probabilities, distributions, and linear regression models, with a review of plotting distributions using histograms in Seaborn, Kernel Density Estimate (KDE), and the Empirical Cumulative Distribution Function (ECDF).

children.title: Introduction to Visualization and Transformations
children.summary: The lecture will emphasize building and visualizing relationships between variables, with a focus on a slower pace than previous sessions. It will address necessary data transformations to create solid datasets and effective visualizations for modeling and stakeholder communication.

children.title: Introduction to Visualization and Transformations
children.summary: The lecture will focus on building and visualizing relationships between variables, emphasizing a slower pace compared to previous sessions. It will cover necessary data transformations to ensure solid datasets and effective visualizations for modeling and stakeholder communication.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So with that added away, we are going into visualization too. So this is the second lecture in this sequence. What I'm planning to do is to go a little bit slower, slower today compared to what we've seen in the previous lectures, kind of and kind of pick a look at how we can build relationships or visualize relationships between variables. And then how can we make sense of different types of transformations that we need to have in order to have a very solid data set and solid visualization moving forward in the modeling and also sharing that broadly in your reports or sharing it with the stakeholders. children.title: Visualization Theory and Data Transformation Principles
children.summary: The lecture covers visualization theory, emphasizing the effective use of information channels in plots and the significance of data transformation for preparing datasets for modeling. Upcoming topics include probabilities, distributions, and linear regression models. It reviews plotting distributions with histograms using Seaborn, explaining how distributions summarize unique values and their occurrences, and discusses Kernel Density Estimate (KDE) for estimating distribution trends and the Empirical Cumulative Distribution Function (ECDF) for determining the probability of data points.

children.title: Visualization Theory and Data Transformation Principles
children.summary: The lecture will cover visualization theory, focusing on effective use of information channels in plots, and the importance of data transformation. Understanding transformation principles is crucial as students will encounter various datasets throughout the semester, requiring them to prepare data for modeling.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: At the end, we're going to comment, talk a little bit about visualization theory, how you can use different information channels on the plot more effectively. And then we'll talk about that at the end. So again, we're going to talk about some kind of visualization principle, then we're going to talk about transformation. And transformation is really important. This is the topic that you will keep seeing throughout the semester, because we'll give you different data sets. You need to make your data sets ready for the modeling piece. Every time you need to think about whether you need to transform your data or not. So make sure that you are solid on understanding the principles behind transformation and why we do transformation before we jump into the modeling phase. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: At the end, we're going to comment, talk a little bit about visualization theory, how you can use different information channels on the plot more effectively. And then we'll talk about that at the end. So again, we're going to talk about some kind of visualization principle, then we're going to talk about transformation. And transformation is really important. This is the topic that you will keep seeing throughout the semester, because we'll give you different data sets. You need to make your data sets ready for the modeling piece. Every time you need to think about whether you need to transform your data or not. So make sure that you are solid on understanding the principles behind transformation and why we do transformation before we jump into the modeling phase.

children.title: Upcoming Topics: Probabilities and Modeling
children.summary: Next week will focus on probabilities, understanding distributions, and sampling frames in datasets. Following that, the course will delve into building and evaluating linear regression models, indicating a progression into more complex topics.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Kind of looking ahead in the next week we're going to come back and talk a little bit about Probabilities and understanding the distribution understanding the sampling frame in your data set and then a week after I'm going to come back and start the modeling lectures. So we're going to De kind of Go deeper into understanding that okay, not that your data is ready. How can you build a linear regression model? How do you evaluate it linear regression model and so on and so forth? So things are going to get Way more interesting as we go not that it was not interesting so far. children.title: Plotting Distributions: Histograms and Density
children.summary: The segment reviews previous content on plotting distributions, specifically histograms, using Seaborn's plot function. It explains that a distribution summarizes unique values and their occurrences, transforming quantitative data into categories (bins) to analyze frequency. It also discusses plotting density on the y-axis, where the total area under the histogram should equal 100% or 1, representing a probability distribution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, talking about our agenda, the first thing is kind of reminding you of what we have seen in a previous lecture. Basically, in a previous lecture, we were just trying to plot distribution. We had seen that we can plot histograms, there were different ways that we could plot histograms. But the last thing that I left you with was that this plot function in seaborn that's kind of a wrapper on top of any kind of visualization that you want to have, on plotting distributions. I remember that the distribution was a way for us to summarize the entire trend in the data. We would basically say that how many unique values we have and how many of each one of those unique values we have, that's a distribution. When we're looking at quantitative variable, we're basically looking at the scale and we're trying to bend our data points into different categories, kind of transforming your quantitative data to a semi-qualitative data and the categories be all of those bins. I'm going to be looking at the frequency of the occurrences of values in each one of those bins, essentially. So this is an example of us. Flooding the histogram and we do that by calling this blood and specifying that the kind is his. And on the y-axis we talked about we can plot different things on the y-axis. We can plot density. If you plot density that means that the area under the curve or the area, the summation of the area for all of those bars should add up to be equal to 100% or be equal to 1 so that it generally shows a probability distribution. leaf_chunk.audio: So, talking about our agenda, the first thing is kind of reminding you of what we have seen in a previous lecture. Basically, in a previous lecture, we were just trying to plot distribution. We had seen that we can plot histograms, there were different ways that we could plot histograms. But the last thing that I left you with was that this plot function in seaborn that's kind of a wrapper on top of any kind of visualization that you want to have, on plotting distributions. I remember that the distribution was a way for us to summarize the entire trend in the data. We would basically say that how many unique values we have and how many of each one of those unique values we have, that's a distribution. When we're looking at quantitative variable, we're basically looking at the scale and we're trying to bend our data points into different categories, kind of transforming your quantitative data to a semi-qualitative data and the categories be all of those bins. I'm going to be looking at the frequency of the occurrences of values in each one of those bins, essentially. So this is an example of us. Flooding the histogram and we do that by calling this blood and specifying that the kind is his. And on the y-axis we talked about we can plot different things on the y-axis. We can plot density. If you plot density that means that the area under the curve or the area, the summation of the area for all of those bars should add up to be equal to 100% or be equal to 1 so that it generally shows a probability distribution.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimate and ECDF Plotting
children.summary: Kernel Density Estimate (KDE) is explained as a method to estimate the distribution trend from data points by placing a kernel over each point, scaling, and summing them to create a smooth curve. The area under the KDE curve equals one, representing a probability function. Additionally, the Empirical Cumulative Distribution Function (ECDF) is introduced, showing the probability of data points being below or equal to a specific value, illustrated with an example related to gross national income.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We could also plot a KDE plot. KDE is then for a kernel-lensity estimate. This is what we saw in the previous lecture. Basically, on each one of the data points, we would put the kernel. We call that the kernel could be any probability function that models one specific data point. We put the kernel on all of the data points. We scale them down. We sum them up so that it gives us this kernel-lensity estimate curve. But basically what it is is that it's an estimation of the trend that you see in the histogram. So if I want to basically go ahead and naively put a curve on top of this histogram that models this distribution, I would arrive at something exactly like that. So think about kernel-lessity estimate as just kind of an estimate that you have so that you don't necessarily need to deal with all of those details of each one of those bars, but what you're having is just looking at the general distribution. What we also plotted on the general y-axis is the density because kernel density estimate is a probability function the area under this kdE should be equal to one. So that's why you see that the skills are matching in this case. started okay we can also go and plot the ECDF. chart, which is empirical cumulative distribution function, which shows that at every given point, for example, here, what's the ratio or what's the What's the probability that any data point is below or equal to that specific point, which in this case is 85% or something of our data points are below, for example, 40,000 GNI. So that's kind of how we try to plot distributions. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: We could also plot a KDE plot. KDE is then for a kernel-lensity estimate. This is what we saw in the previous lecture. Basically, on each one of the data points, we would put the kernel. We call that the kernel could be any probability function that models one specific data point. We put the kernel on all of the data points. We scale them down. We sum them up so that it gives us this kernel-lensity estimate curve. But basically what it is is that it's an estimation of the trend that you see in the histogram. So if I want to basically go ahead and naively put a curve on top of this histogram that models this distribution, I would arrive at something exactly like that. So think about kernel-lessity estimate as just kind of an estimate that you have so that you don't necessarily need to deal with all of those details of each one of those bars, but what you're having is just looking at the general distribution. What we also plotted on the general y-axis is the density because kernel density estimate is a probability function the area under this kdE should be equal to one. So that's why you see that the skills are matching in this case. started okay we can also go and plot the ECDF. chart, which is empirical cumulative distribution function, which shows that at every given point, for example, here, what's the ratio or what's the What's the probability that any data point is below or equal to that specific point, which in this case is 85% or something of our data points are below, for example, 40,000 GNI. So that's kind of how we try to plot distributions.

children.title: Scatter Plots and Regression Techniques Overview
children.summary: Scatter plots model relationships between two variables, highlighting trends that may be linear, non-linear, or semi-linear, and are crucial for regression analysis, emphasizing that correlation does not imply causation. Matplotlib and Seaborn are tools for plotting, with Seaborn allowing for categorical representation via the hue parameter. Addressing overplotting can be achieved through jittering, which enhances visibility without losing trend information. Effective data visualization is essential for evaluating data readiness, particularly in analyzing GNI and adult literacy rates, while generalizability and transformations are necessary for accurate trend representation in data modeling, addressing challenges like outliers and differing scales through normalization.

children.title: Modeling Relationships: Scatter Plots and Regression Techniques
children.summary: Scatter plots are used to model relationships between two variables, revealing trends that can be linear, non-linear, or semi-linear, and are essential in regression analysis to assess potential linear relationships while noting that correlation does not imply causation. Matplotlib and Seaborn offer similar plotting structures, with Seaborn enabling categorical representation through the hue parameter. Overplotting in scatter plots can be addressed by jittering, which adds random noise to data points for improved visibility without losing trend information.

children.title: Modeling Relationships: Scatter Plots and Regression
children.summary: The focus is on modeling relationships between variables using scatter plots, which illustrate associations between two variables like x1 and x2, revealing trends that can be linear, non-linear, or semi-linear. Scatter plots are crucial in regression for assessing potential linear relationships, helping to differentiate between simple linear and non-linear patterns, while emphasizing that correlation does not imply causation.

children.title: Modeling Relationships Between Variables
children.summary: The focus shifts from visualizing distributions to modeling relationships between multiple variables, particularly through scatter plots. Scatter plots illustrate the association between two variables, such as x1 and x2, showing trends that can be linear, non-linear, or semi-linear, as well as various shapes like V-shaped relationships.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But moving away from distributions, what if we want to model the relationship between multiple different variables as opposed to is just looking at one variable. So what we can do is something that you had seen before in data 8. In data 8, you had seen a scatter plots. And scatter plots were kind of showing that what's the association between two different variables. So for example, here I call this variable x1, this variable, or this dimension x2. And for every given data point, it's the relationship between x1 and x2 that you're plotting as a single point on the plot. Usually with a scatter plot, we could basically plot the entire data set or the association between X1 and X2 variables, and it will show us the trend. So in this case, for example, you're seeing that I have a simple linear kind of trend in the data. I can also observe non-linear trend. I can also see kind of a semi-linear trend, but it's kind of spreading as X grows or kind of V shape relationship between two variables. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: But moving away from distributions, what if we want to model the relationship between multiple different variables as opposed to is just looking at one variable. So what we can do is something that you had seen before in data 8. In data 8, you had seen a scatter plots. And scatter plots were kind of showing that what's the association between two different variables. So for example, here I call this variable x1, this variable, or this dimension x2. And for every given data point, it's the relationship between x1 and x2 that you're plotting as a single point on the plot. Usually with a scatter plot, we could basically plot the entire data set or the association between X1 and X2 variables, and it will show us the trend. So in this case, for example, you're seeing that I have a simple linear kind of trend in the data. I can also observe non-linear trend. I can also see kind of a semi-linear trend, but it's kind of spreading as X grows or kind of V shape relationship between two variables.

children.title: Importance of Scatter Plots in Regression
children.summary: Scatter plots visualize the relationship between two numerical variables, aiding in the assessment of potential linear relationships for linear regression. They help determine how to model these relationships, distinguishing between simple linear and non-linear patterns. It's important to remember that correlation does not imply causation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Why is it important? to visualize two variables with respect to each other. It's mostly important because when we go into linear regression and we have a target variable that we want to predict. And we want to predict that using some input information or some input feature. We might want to visualize it in order to see if they actually for fact have linear relationship with each other or not. So that's part of the reason that we might want to use kind of visualization of the relationship between two different variables in a sense. So how do we plot the scatter plots? You've seen map plot lab, you've seen seaborne. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Comparing Matplotlib and Seaborn; Scatter Plot Techniques
children.summary: Matplotlib and Seaborn share a similar structure for plotting, utilizing sequences of x and y values, with Seaborn allowing for categorical representation through the hue parameter. Scatter plots visualize relationships between two quantitative variables, but may suffer from overplotting, which can be mitigated by jittering—adding random noise to data points to enhance visibility while preserving trends. Techniques for adding noise to data points are discussed to improve scatter plot clarity.

children.title: Comparing Matplotlib and Seaborn for Plotting
children.summary: The structure for calling plotting functions in Matplotlib and Seaborn is similar, with both requiring sequences of x and y values. In Seaborn, specific plotting functions like scatter plots can be used, where the x and y axes are defined by column names, and an optional hue parameter can be added to represent categories through color. An example is given of plotting the percentage of gross per capita against female adult literacy rate, categorized by continents, with plans to address issues and summarize trends using various plot types.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Effectively, the structure of how you can call each one of the plotting functions in map plot lab and seaborne is very similar. So remember that what we had was with the map plot lab, we could just call the plotting function and we would pass the sequences that need to be plotted. So for example, in this case, I have, I can pass a sequence, which means it can be an array, it can be a series, it can be a list object of x values and y values that need will be plotted. With this, with the C-born, the relationship with this was slightly different. You just need to remember about plotting function. What's the name of that plotting function that will do the thing that you want? So here in this case is the scatter plot. column name, so that's the string value. The column name that goes on the x-axis, the column name that goes on the y-axis. And then if you're feeling fancy, we could also say that there is a third dimension that I want you to plot and that's the color. So, hue references the color, which basically means that, okay, depending on different categories of values that you have in the column that's called hue column, go ahead and color different stuff. And in that case, you're effectively using tree information channels. X information channel, Y information channel, and also hew. So you're using the color as well. So if I go ahead and just plot a scatter plot using the seaboard version, for example, plotting percentage of gross per capita and also female adult literacy rate. And I also add the hew to be different continents. This is kind of the plot that I'll get. And while keep working on this plot, we'll see different issues with this plot. We'll try to solve it, and then we'll try to also summarize all of these trends that you see using other types of plots. And then we'll see what kind of events, kind of similar plots that we can use in order to show the relationship between two different variables. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: Effectively, the structure of how you can call each one of the plotting functions in map plot lab and seaborne is very similar. So remember that what we had was with the map plot lab, we could just call the plotting function and we would pass the sequences that need to be plotted. So for example, in this case, I have, I can pass a sequence, which means it can be an array, it can be a series, it can be a list object of x values and y values that need will be plotted. With this, with the C-born, the relationship with this was slightly different. You just need to remember about plotting function. What's the name of that plotting function that will do the thing that you want? So here in this case is the scatter plot. column name, so that's the string value. The column name that goes on the x-axis, the column name that goes on the y-axis. And then if you're feeling fancy, we could also say that there is a third dimension that I want you to plot and that's the color. So, hue references the color, which basically means that, okay, depending on different categories of values that you have in the column that's called hue column, go ahead and color different stuff. And in that case, you're effectively using tree information channels. X information channel, Y information channel, and also hew. So you're using the color as well. So if I go ahead and just plot a scatter plot using the seaboard version, for example, plotting percentage of gross per capita and also female adult literacy rate. And I also add the hew to be different continents. This is kind of the plot that I'll get. And while keep working on this plot, we'll see different issues with this plot. We'll try to solve it, and then we'll try to also summarize all of these trends that you see using other types of plots. And then we'll see what kind of events, kind of similar plots that we can use in order to show the relationship between two different variables.

children.title: Scatter Plots: Relationships and Overplotting
children.summary: Scatter plots illustrate relationships between two quantitative variables by plotting one on the x-axis and the other on the y-axis, with each point representing a data point. A common issue in scatter plots is overplotting, where points overlap and become indistinguishable. Jittering can be applied to add random noise to the data points, improving visibility while maintaining the overall trends.

leaf_chunk.text:

So what we had in the previous plot was that there was one major issue and you'll keep hearing from me that I'll say that okay this plot has a lot of overplotting I can see this is not a good plot. children.title: Overplotting in Scatter Plots and Remedies
children.summary: Overplotting occurs when multiple data points are stacked in the same area of a scatter plot, making it difficult to discern trends. One remedy is jittering, which involves adding random noise to the x and y values to slightly separate the points while maintaining overall trends. Scatter plots are used to visualize relationships between two quantitative variables, with each point representing a data point in the dataset.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What overplotting essentially means is that if I go back to the previous slide you will see that there is a lot of these different points that are basically clumped together in that top right corner and I don't like that because I can't tell. tell apart what's happening in that area. It can figure out is there a specific trend for a specific continent or not? So it's just that I don't want to see points plotted on top of each other. So that's called overplotting. There are different remedies that we have for overplotting. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Jittering to Reduce Overplotting in Scatter Plots
children.summary: To address overplotting in scatter plots, random noise can be added to both the x and y dimensions of data points. This technique, known as jittering, allows for better visibility of individual data points while maintaining the overall trends. An example involves creating uniform random noise for the x and y values and plotting the adjusted data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There is one simple way is that we would just add random noise. If we add random noise, for example, in this case, I would say that I'm adding random noise to the X dimension of all of the data points. So you can see that for all of the data points, I'm looking at the lengths of WB data frame, which is essentially the number of rows that I have, right? in the in the word bank data frame that we started using in the last lecture and I'm also creating a noise kind of less or no set of values for the wide dimension and what we'll do here is that we'll say that as you're creating this a scatter plot please go ahead and the noise That we created on the x-axis to the x-dimension and also please add the noise that we created for the y-axis to the y-axis dimension and then plot the values. children.title: Adding Noise and Jitter to Data Plots
children.summary: Noise is added to data points to prevent overlap, using a random generator function that creates values with a uniform distribution between -1 and 1. The y-axis range is adjusted to -5 to 5, and point sizes are set to 15 to enhance visibility. While adding noise can introduce inaccuracies, disclaimers can clarify that the data is jittered, allowing for better interpretation of trends.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay, so the idea is that because we're adding noise and because noise is randomly generated from a certain distribution. It will help us. of move points apart from each other so that they're not exactly flooded on top of each other. What is this NP random uniform? So this is a random generator function, which essentially what we're doing here is that we're saying that go ahead. Create a value randomly and generate that with uniform distribution between the range of negative one and one. And then how many I want I want for each one of the numbers so the length of the number of random randomly generated variables or values that you're creating should be the length of the word bang data frame. So that's where the x-axis for the y-axis I'm saying that now your wrench can be wider it's going between negative negative 5 and 5 because the range of the y-axis is slightly different than the range of the x-axis. We need to jitter a little bit more so that we see the differences in the plot. So that's basically what's happening. The other way in order to make sure that the points are not on top of each other is to change the marking size. So here I'm saying that the size of each one of those dots that you're putting, let that be 15. And 15 is a smaller than the normal, valid, and it says it will also help to some extent. But these are kind of general ideas that we're using. So that the plot that you're getting at the end, you can interpret it. So the question is then going to be that if you're adding in noise, is that going to create inacurus in the plot? Or if you're trying to kind of show some trend to a mistake holder by adding in noise, you're basically kind of... uh false define the the underlying data and the answer is yes sort of but if you do that uh and and this and kind of put a disclaimer that the data is jitter so that you don't have overplodding that's fine and then people would know that okay the data is not exactly as is plotted but we can basically see the trend of very majority of the data is plotted versus not. It will help us. of move points apart from each other so that they're not exactly flooded on top of each other. What is this NP random uniform? So this is a random generator function, which essentially what we're doing here is that we're saying that go ahead. Create a value randomly and generate that with uniform distribution between the range of negative one and one. And then how many I want I want for each one of the numbers so the length of the number of random randomly generated variables or values that you're creating should be the length of the word bang data frame. So that's where the x-axis for the y-axis I'm saying that now your wrench can be wider it's going between negative negative 5 and 5 because the range of the y-axis is slightly different than the range of the x-axis. We need to jitter a little bit more so that we see the differences in the plot. So that's basically what's happening. The other way in order to make sure that the points are not on top of each other is to change the marking size. So here I'm saying that the size of each one of those dots that you're putting, let that be 15. And 15 is a smaller than the normal, valid, and it says it will also help to some extent. But these are kind of general ideas that we're using. So that the plot that you're getting at the end, you can interpret it. So the question is then going to be that if you're adding in noise, is that going to create inacurus in the plot? Or if you're trying to kind of show some trend to a mistake holder by adding in noise, you're basically kind of... uh false define the the underlying data and the answer is yes sort of but if you do that uh and and this and kind of put a disclaimer that the data is jitter so that you don't have overplodding that's fine and then people would know that okay the data is not exactly as is plotted but we can basically see the trend of very majority of the data is plotted versus not.

children.title: LM Plot: Linear Regression Visualization
children.summary: The LM plot is a useful function in Seaborn for visualizing linear regression by combining a scatter plot with a regression line. It predicts the Y dimension based on the X dimension and includes a 95% confidence interval to indicate the range where data points may fall when modeling the trend.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, in an attempt to also understand the relationships better, and in order to also mitigate a little bit of this overplotting that we have, we also have some other plotting functions. So, the first plotting function that is very interesting and you're going to see that in linear regression lectures is called LM plot. So, it's basically a plot that plots the linear regression, it plots the scatter plot, and then it will also put the linear regression line through the data. It will assume that you want to use the X dimension in order to predict the Y dimension. It will find the trend, the linear trend, and it will put that. You're not saying that exactly, but there is this. kind of confidence interval or this cone area which will show you that okay this is the trend and this is our 95% confidence interval for where the data points could be if you're modeling the trend using this linear regression so those are kind of the lines and the small confidence interval that you see on the plot as well. leaf_chunk.audio: So, in an attempt to also understand the relationships better, and in order to also mitigate a little bit of this overplotting that we have, we also have some other plotting functions. So, the first plotting function that is very interesting and you're going to see that in linear regression lectures is called LM plot. So, it's basically a plot that plots the linear regression, it plots the scatter plot, and then it will also put the linear regression line through the data. It will assume that you want to use the X dimension in order to predict the Y dimension. It will find the trend, the linear trend, and it will put that. You're not saying that exactly, but there is this. kind of confidence interval or this cone area which will show you that okay this is the trend and this is our 95% confidence interval for where the data points could be if you're modeling the trend using this linear regression so those are kind of the lines and the small confidence interval that you see on the plot as well.

children.title: Seaborn LM plot and joint plot overview
children.summary: The LM plot function in Seaborn allows users to plot data by specifying the desired columns. The joint plot creates a joint distribution using a scatter plot along with histograms for the x and y axes, providing insights into the distribution of data points and helping to visualize overplotting.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the function exactly you call LM plot, you pass the data from, you pass the columns that you want to be plotted, the names, and it will plot that for you. The other interesting plot that I like is also joint plot. So joint plot, there are different versions of joint plot, kind of similar to this plot that you can plot different kinds of this plot. Here you can also plot different kinds of joint plot, but this is the most basic one. What it does is it will create the joint distribution using the scatter plot, and it will also plot the histogram of the distribution of the individual x-axis and individual y-axis on the corners as well. So here, for example, you're seeing that it shows the histogram or the distribution of the y-axis here on the y-scale, and then this is the distribution of x-axis on the x-axis. So this will help you to some extent figure out how many data points you have here. Although you have overplotting, but this will help you kind of see that, okay, now this is how many data points I have in that corner, and this is also how many data points I have in this top part of the plot. leaf_chunk.audio: So the function exactly you call LM plot, you pass the data from, you pass the columns that you want to be plotted, the names, and it will plot that for you. The other interesting plot that I like is also joint plot. So joint plot, there are different versions of joint plot, kind of similar to this plot that you can plot different kinds of this plot. Here you can also plot different kinds of joint plot, but this is the most basic one. What it does is it will create the joint distribution using the scatter plot, and it will also plot the histogram of the distribution of the individual x-axis and individual y-axis on the corners as well. So here, for example, you're seeing that it shows the histogram or the distribution of the y-axis here on the y-scale, and then this is the distribution of x-axis on the x-axis. So this will help you to some extent figure out how many data points you have here. Although you have overplotting, but this will help you kind of see that, okay, now this is how many data points I have in that corner, and this is also how many data points I have in this top part of the plot.

children.title: Joint Plot: Hex Plot Visualization
children.summary: Joint plots help visualize individual and joint distributions using scatter plots. An alternative is the hex plot, which clusters data points into hexagons and colors them based on the number of points in each area, aiding in avoiding overplotting and clearly showing distributions. The color gradient from dark blue to white indicates the density of data points, with darker colors representing higher concentrations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this kind of helps you understand the individual distributions as well as the joint distribution using this scatter plot. So the syntax is exactly So you call joint plot, past the data frame, past the column names, and you'll be on your way. There are other different fancy versions of Joint Plot. So this is a Joint Plot where I can say that the kind of the thing that I want you to plot in the middle is not a scatter plot, but give me the hex plot. So here I'm saying that the kind is equal to hex. What it is is essentially, we'll try to clump. different data points into hexagons and then it will color hexagons based on the number of data points that are present in each one of those locations. So if we take a look at this previous part, you're seeing that I have a high distribution somewhere around here. And then this is the highest distribution and then slightly lower distribution here and then the lowest distribution is going to be here and here. So we all start with the dark blue in the hexagons where we have the most distribution and then lighter blue lighter blue up until yellow up until white when there is no data point present in those areas, which is what you're seeing we did not have any data point here so we don't have. The hexagons are colored as white and then we have majority of the distribution here so that's why it's a dark blue hexagon in those areas. So it will basically help you to avoid overplotting but it will also help you to see the distribution because now that the data points are not on top of each other and I'm just counting them and I'm coloring a region based on that. That's all you need as a that's what you need as a viewer of this plot just know the distribution. Using the same strategy, we could go a little bit fancier and instead of just doing the counting or doing the coloring based on the counts, we could do it based on the KVE. leaf_chunk.audio: So this kind of helps you understand the individual distributions as well as the joint distribution using this scatter plot. So the syntax is exactly So you call joint plot, past the data frame, past the column names, and you'll be on your way. There are other different fancy versions of Joint Plot. So this is a Joint Plot where I can say that the kind of the thing that I want you to plot in the middle is not a scatter plot, but give me the hex plot. So here I'm saying that the kind is equal to hex. What it is is essentially, we'll try to clump. different data points into hexagons and then it will color hexagons based on the number of data points that are present in each one of those locations. So if we take a look at this previous part, you're seeing that I have a high distribution somewhere around here. And then this is the highest distribution and then slightly lower distribution here and then the lowest distribution is going to be here and here. So we all start with the dark blue in the hexagons where we have the most distribution and then lighter blue lighter blue up until yellow up until white when there is no data point present in those areas, which is what you're seeing we did not have any data point here so we don't have. The hexagons are colored as white and then we have majority of the distribution here so that's why it's a dark blue hexagon in those areas. So it will basically help you to avoid overplotting but it will also help you to see the distribution because now that the data points are not on top of each other and I'm just counting them and I'm coloring a region based on that. That's all you need as a that's what you need as a viewer of this plot just know the distribution. Using the same strategy, we could go a little bit fancier and instead of just doing the counting or doing the coloring based on the counts, we could do it based on the KVE.

children.title: Contour Plots: KDE Visualization Techniques
children.summary: A contour plot serves as a 2D representation of a Kernel Density Estimate (KDE) plot, akin to a topographic map where contour lines indicate regions with equal data density. Darker colors on the plot signify areas with a higher concentration of data points, while lighter colors represent lower densities. The sns.kdeplot function can be utilized in Python to create these visualizations, enhancing the understanding of data distribution across two dimensions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So assume that you have all of those points or different indicators for each one of these points in the 2D surface. You put a KDE plot on top of it and you color it based on, for example, the height of those KDE kind of or that KDE function, which essentially is going to tell you that, okay, this is where I have the tallest KDE kind of point or the curve and then as I'm going away into different then the color is going to be lighter blue and then in these areas I have none data point. children.title: 2D KDE Plots: Implementation and Interpretation
children.summary: The segment discusses the implementation of a 2D Kernel Density Estimate (KDE) plot using a data frame with two columns. It highlights the importance of coloring the contours to better visualize data density, where darker regions indicate higher concentrations of data points. Additionally, it addresses alternatives to overplotting, such as using scatterplots with jitter or focusing on trends rather than individual data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's basically the point that the KDE curve is going to all the way to zero. Right? So this is a 2D KDE version of what we did have before. The way that you can implement it as exactly as what how we would call the KDE plot. Here I would say that I have a KDE plot, but instead of only passing one data point or one column, I would say that I have a data frame and I'm passing you two different columns. So just go ahead and plot it or plot the KDE plot in two dimension using all of these contours that we're seeing. color so I can tell the differences a little bit better. Otherwise it will just give you the outline of these contours and it won't color the inside so you won't feel the color. So that's kind of the stylistic kind of choice that we made. And if you take a look at where we have the darker regions or darker contours, it's exactly corresponding to where we have most of the points. So it's pretty interesting. But notice that if I had the issue of overplotting, I could just stick with the scatterplot, jitter it in place, and just make sense of what exists in the data, or I could go one level advance and then say that I don't care about individual data points, but I want to see the trend. leaf_chunk.audio: So that's basically the point that the KDE curve is going to all the way to zero. Right? So this is a 2D KDE version of what we did have before. The way that you can implement it as exactly as what how we would call the KDE plot. Here I would say that I have a KDE plot, but instead of only passing one data point or one column, I would say that I have a data frame and I'm passing you two different columns. So just go ahead and plot it or plot the KDE plot in two dimension using all of these contours that we're seeing. color so I can tell the differences a little bit better. Otherwise it will just give you the outline of these contours and it won't color the inside so you won't feel the color. So that's kind of the stylistic kind of choice that we made. And if you take a look at where we have the darker regions or darker contours, it's exactly corresponding to where we have most of the points. So it's pretty interesting. But notice that if I had the issue of overplotting, I could just stick with the scatterplot, jitter it in place, and just make sense of what exists in the data, or I could go one level advance and then say that I don't care about individual data points, but I want to see the trend.

children.title: Contour Plots and Visualization Tools Overview
children.summary: Contour plots are a 2-dimensional version of KDE plots, resembling topographic maps where contour lines indicate areas of equal data density, with darker colors representing higher data concentrations. Various tools for visualizing distributions include rug plots, histograms, density plots, box plots, and violin plots for single variables, and scatter plots, hex plots, and contour plots for joint distributions. The course primarily utilizes seaborn and matplotlib, with plotly also being introduced for interactive visualizations.

children.summary: Various tools for visualizing distributions include rug plots, histograms, density plots, box plots, and violin plots for single variable distributions, while scatter plots, hex plots, and contour plots are used for joint distributions of two quantitative variables. The course primarily utilizes seaborn and matplotlib, with pandas also offering basic plotting methods. Additionally, plotly is mentioned as an interactive plotting library that may be used in lectures and assignments.

children.title: Interactive Plotting and Visualization Tools
children.summary: Visualization requires careful consideration of various tools and methods, including rug plots, histograms, density plots, box plots, and violin plots for single-variable distributions, as well as scatter plots, hex plots, and contour plots for joint distributions. The course primarily utilizes seaborn and matplotlib, with pandas offering basic plotting capabilities. Additionally, plotly is introduced as a library for creating interactive plots, which will be used in lectures, labs, and assignments.

children.title: Visualization Techniques and Tools Overview
children.summary: Visualization requires careful consideration of the type of plot based on the data being analyzed. For single variable distributions, options include rug plots, histograms, density plots, box plots, and violin plots. For joint distributions of two quantitative variables, scatter plots, hex plots, and contour plots are recommended. The course primarily utilizes seaborn and matplotlib, while pandas offers basic plotting methods; plotly is also mentioned as a tool for creating interactive plots.

children.title: Importance of Visualization in Data Transformation
children.summary: Visualization is crucial for understanding data readiness for modeling, identifying outliers, and determining necessary transformations. It serves two main purposes: aiding personal comprehension of data and effectively communicating results, such as model performance metrics. Additionally, visualization helps assess whether data trends can be linearized.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But the next thing that I want to talk about is about transformation. This is a topic that I said is going to be very important for when we're going into the modeling phase. Kind of going back into why we want to do visualization remember that we wanted to do visualization as part of the EDA. So if you want to make it take a look at your data and then figure out if your data is ready for the modeling phase, if your data that has any outlier or not, if the data needs any transformation or not, these are all the reasons that we might want to use visualization. Additionally, a lot of time we want to communicate results. We'll ask you that, okay, do this modeling and then plot, for example, the distribution of your. The root and the score error of your model so that's something that you want to communicate with us in your homework and whatnot so these are the two reasons that we might want to be we might want to do visualization and a lot of time what we're interested in doing is to figure out if our data is linearized or not and in order to find trends in our data. leaf_chunk.audio: But the next thing that I want to talk about is about transformation. This is a topic that I said is going to be very important for when we're going into the modeling phase. Kind of going back into why we want to do visualization remember that we wanted to do visualization as part of the EDA. So if you want to make it take a look at your data and then figure out if your data is ready for the modeling phase, if your data that has any outlier or not, if the data needs any transformation or not, these are all the reasons that we might want to use visualization. Additionally, a lot of time we want to communicate results. We'll ask you that, okay, do this modeling and then plot, for example, the distribution of your. The root and the score error of your model so that's something that you want to communicate with us in your homework and whatnot so these are the two reasons that we might want to be we might want to do visualization and a lot of time what we're interested in doing is to figure out if our data is linearized or not and in order to find trends in our data.

children.title: Data Visualization and Modeling Techniques
children.summary: Effective data visualization is vital for assessing data readiness, particularly in analyzing the relationship between Gross National Income (GNI) and adult literacy rates. Generalizability in data modeling ensures accurate representation of trends across datasets, while transformations help establish linear relationships necessary for linear regression. Challenges such as outliers and differing scales on axes complicate modeling, necessitating normalization and adjustments to maintain data integrity.

children.title: Data Visualization: GNI and Literacy Rate Analysis
children.summary: Effective data visualization is crucial for assessing data readiness for modeling, as it helps identify issues like overplotting that obscure trends. Analyzing the relationship between Gross National Income (GNI) and adult literacy rates illustrates the significance of visualization in understanding and communicating data insights.

children.title: Data Visualization: GNI and Literacy Rate Analysis
children.summary: Visualization helps assess data readiness for modeling by identifying issues like overplotting, where data points cluster in one area, making trends difficult to discern. For instance, examining the relationship between Gross National Income (GNI) and adult literacy rates highlights the importance of effective visualization in understanding and communicating data insights.

children.title: Generalizability in Data Modeling
children.summary: Generalizability in data modeling refers to a model's ability to accurately represent trends across the entire dataset. A model assuming a vertical trend line implies that adult illiteracy rates are independent of GNI, which may overlook data diversity. It is crucial to develop models that consider all data characteristics to prevent inaccuracies and outliers.

children.title: Generalizability in Data Modeling
children.summary: Generalizability refers to the ability of a model to accurately represent trends across the entire dataset. A model that assumes a vertical trend line suggests that adult illiteracy rates do not depend on GNI, which may not capture the diversity of data points. It is essential to create models that account for all data characteristics to avoid inaccuracies and outliers.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we don't want the data to be smushed together in one specific area. The other issue that we have is that with this data it's really hard to have a model that's generalizable. We're going to talk about generalizability, I love it in the modeling lectures, but basically what we mean is that if I want to go ahead and model for the trend in this data. Just basically looking at the distribution of this data I would I might for example think that okay the trend in the data is going to be kind of this vertical kind of line that basically says that depending on So that would say that the adult illiteracy rate does not depend on the GNI. So if you increase GNI, for example here, then technically your adult illiteracy rate is not going to change that much because most of your data is on this vertical line. So because of that This model of me thinking that the trend is this vertical line is not going to be generalizable because there is a lot of different data points and the right hand side that I didn't capture or did a model. So we want to have a model that... As accurately as possible captures the trend or the characteristics of all of the data points as much as possible and unabashed has a good accuracy on the entire data set. So because of that we don't necessarily want to have some outliers that are outlying here that we can't necessarily model them. that this data is not ready for being used in a linear regression or any modeling test that we want to have. leaf_chunk.audio: So we don't want the data to be smushed together in one specific area. The other issue that we have is that with this data it's really hard to have a model that's generalizable. We're going to talk about generalizability, I love it in the modeling lectures, but basically what we mean is that if I want to go ahead and model for the trend in this data. Just basically looking at the distribution of this data I would I might for example think that okay the trend in the data is going to be kind of this vertical kind of line that basically says that depending on So that would say that the adult illiteracy rate does not depend on the GNI. So if you increase GNI, for example here, then technically your adult illiteracy rate is not going to change that much because most of your data is on this vertical line. So because of that This model of me thinking that the trend is this vertical line is not going to be generalizable because there is a lot of different data points and the right hand side that I didn't capture or did a model. So we want to have a model that... As accurately as possible captures the trend or the characteristics of all of the data points as much as possible and unabashed has a good accuracy on the entire data set. So because of that we don't necessarily want to have some outliers that are outlying here that we can't necessarily model them. that this data is not ready for being used in a linear regression or any modeling test that we want to have.

children.title: Data Transformation and Linearization Techniques
children.summary: Transformations are necessary to establish a linear relationship between Gross National Income (GNI) and adult literacy rates, enhancing interpretation through proper scaling and feature generation. Linearization simplifies the understanding of variable relationships, crucial for linear regression models, and allows for effective modeling. Techniques such as raising the y-dimension to the fourth power can help linearize data, while addressing issues like outliers and overplotting is essential for accurate visualization and modeling.

children.title: Data Transformation for Linear Relationships
children.summary: Transformations are necessary to create a linear relationship between Gross National Income (GNI) and the adult literacy rate. The visual representation shows a general linear trend captured by a red line, indicating improved interpretation compared to previous vertical line representations. Proper data scaling and feature generation are crucial for effective visualization.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But instead, what we need to do is we still need to transform the data, do more feature in generating so that the data is ready so that now there is kind of a linear relationship between GNI and also the transform version of GNI and the transform version of the adult will literacy rate. Stop! What we want is exactly going from left hand side to the right hand side. What you're seeing is that the scales on both of the axes are now different. Now there is a general linear trend that is captured using that red linear line. And then you can see that generally this captures the trend more or less better than that vertical line that I had initially or I thought about initially. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Importance of Linearization in Data Transformation
children.summary: Linearization of data is essential for establishing a linear relationship between dimensions, which is crucial for linear regression models. This transformation simplifies the interpretation of relationships between variables, allowing for effective modeling and understanding.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay? So what we're essentially doing in data 100 when we talk about you need to do transformation is that we're trying to linearize the data, linearize the association between. One dimension versus another dimension. Why do we care about linearization and not any other kind of transformation? For one is that we can do this transform versions could be used in a linear regression model, which is the first model that you're seeing in data 100. Another reason is that a lot of time when you want to understand the relationship between two variables, it is modeled best with a simple linear regression explanation. children.title: Linearization: Transforming Data for Analysis
children.summary: To analyze the relationship between variables, data often needs to be linearized, allowing for a linear association between transformed x and y dimensions. Linear relationships simplify interpretation, enabling the use of slopes and intercepts to understand variable connections. Upcoming sessions will focus on building linear models, which are more effective with linearized data.

children.title: Transformations for Linearizing Data Relationships
children.summary: To achieve a linear relationship between variables, transformations such as raising the y-dimension to the fourth power can be applied. Issues like outliers and overplotting, particularly with large x values and clustered y values, can distort the model's accuracy, necessitating careful consideration of these factors in data visualization and modeling.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And how did I actually decide that, okay, I need to do a lot transformation on the x-axis and I need to actually calculate y dimension to the power of four so that then now the association between these two the transformations are more or less linear. So we're going to do kind of an experiment and then figure out that okay if there are different issues in the plot, what kind of transformation should we do in order to solve each and individual issues that we have seen in the in a specific kind of trend or association between two variables and you can do this exercise in the homeworks and every time that you're seeing kind of a relevant issue in the future. So, what makes this plot? Non-linear or that we don't like the way that is plotted and it has overplodding. It has data points that are kind of outlier is one piece that we have large outlying x values. Remember I said that okay if I'm trying to model this trend and I go ahead and say that okay my model is something like this. There's no way that I can model these outliers accurately. So I call them outliers and I want I don't want those really large values kind of influencing the model accuracy or whatever model that I'm building. So that's the first issue. The other issue that we have is that we have a lot of those large vi values in the top left corner that are essentially clumped together. So that's an example of overplating. We know it. We see it. So we need to address it. So why would we care about each one of these issues? Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: And how did I actually decide that, okay, I need to do a lot transformation on the x-axis and I need to actually calculate y dimension to the power of four so that then now the association between these two the transformations are more or less linear. So we're going to do kind of an experiment and then figure out that okay if there are different issues in the plot, what kind of transformation should we do in order to solve each and individual issues that we have seen in the in a specific kind of trend or association between two variables and you can do this exercise in the homeworks and every time that you're seeing kind of a relevant issue in the future. It has data points that are kind of outlier is one piece that we have large outlying x values. Remember I said that okay if I'm trying to model this trend and I go ahead and say that okay my model is something like this. There's no way that I can model these outliers accurately. So I call them outliers and I want I don't want those really large values kind of influencing the model accuracy or whatever model that I'm building. So that's the first issue. The other issue that we have is that we have a lot of those large vi values in the top left corner that are essentially clumped together. So that's an example of overplating. We know it. We see it. children.title: Challenges in Modeling Outliers in Data
children.summary: Outliers complicate modeling by distorting data distributions, necessitating adjustments towards the main data cluster. A significant issue occurs when differing scales on the x-axis and y-axis lead to misrepresentation, especially when large outlying x values impact the horizontal axis and many large y values compress the vertical axis.

children.title: Challenges in Modeling Outliers in Data
children.summary: Outliers complicate modeling as they distort data distributions, making it essential to adjust them towards the main data cluster. A specific issue arises when the scales of the x-axis and y-axis differ, causing misrepresentation of the data, particularly when large outlying x values affect the horizontal axis and many large y values compress the vertical axis.

children.title: Data Normalization and Transformations in Regression
children.summary: Data normalization is essential for linear regression to ensure adequate spacing of data points, revealing meaningful relationships. Addressing outliers involves scaling the X dimension to achieve a uniform spread, while log and square root transformations help reduce large values more significantly than smaller ones. Log transformations improve the visibility of data points by minimizing the impact of extreme values, and power transformations can further distinguish closely clustered numbers.

children.title: Importance of Data Normalization in Regression
children.summary: Normalization of dimensions is crucial for optimal performance in linear regression models. Clumping of data points in either the x or y dimensions can obscure distinctions between values, making it difficult to interpret relationships. It is essential to ensure that data points are adequately spaced to reveal meaningful differences, particularly when analyzing variations in y for the same or different x values.

leaf_chunk.text:

So if I want to fit the best fit of the data basically here, it essentially says that For the same value of x if the data points are really kind of clumped together in this vertical axis. For the same x value, I have multiple different y values and those y values are different, right? And basically, I want to see a distinction between them so that as I change x value or as I keep the same x value, I can see the distinction between them. I don't want to have multiple different values really smushed together at the top for the same x or for different values of x. Even for example, if you take a look at, for example, this point right here and this point right here, The x values are entirely different, but then the y values are exactly the same. If you could make a difference between them so that one of them is set at the top of the other one, then you could see a difference between them better. That, okay, when x is different, then there is this light difference in y, so things are going to make more sense. leaf_chunk.audio: That's the separate issue. Yes, so that the scale thing we're going to come back to that in the linear regression lectures that you really need to make sure that our dimensions are always normalized for the best performance of to get the best performance of a linear regression model. Did you want to add something? So if I want to fit the best fit of the data basically here, it essentially says that For the same value of x if the data points are really kind of clumped together in this vertical axis. For the same x value, I have multiple different y values and those y values are different, right? And basically, I want to see a distinction between them so that as I change x value or as I keep the same x value, I can see the distinction between them. I don't want to have multiple different values really smushed together at the top for the same x or for different values of x. Even for example, if you take a look at, for example, this point right here and this point right here, The x values are entirely different, but then the y values are exactly the same. If you could make a difference between them so that one of them is set at the top of the other one, then you could see a difference between them better. That, okay, when x is different, then there is this light difference in y, so things are going to make more sense. So that's the reason that we don't want generally to have the data smushed in the x dimension, or in the y dimension, or in any area in the plot.

children.title: Addressing Outliers in Data Distribution
children.summary: The process of fixing issues related to outliers in data distributions is discussed, focusing on scaling the X dimension to reduce the impact of outliers. A transformed version of the X dimension is aimed to achieve a more uniform spread across the axis, addressing the distortion caused by a few large outlying values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we need to fix those of these issues. But I'm going to basically try to spell out what's the process of fixing each one of those issues. How can I fix this issue of having all of these data points be kind of outlier in the distribution in the right hand side? How can I make sure that my X dimension is kind of scaled in a way that these data points are going to come closer to the rest of the data points? And then basically now I have the transform version of the X dimension to be kind of more uniformly spread out in the X axis as opposed to some of them kind of here and then very few in the outlier in the right hand side.
