children.title: Log and Square Root Transformations Explained
children.summary: Log and square root transformations can significantly reduce large values while having a smaller proportional effect on smaller values, thus bringing data points closer together. For instance, log transformation decreases a large value like 70,000 to a more manageable range, while square root transformation reduces values like 1600 to 40 and 16 to 4, demonstrating that larger values are impacted more significantly.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The kind of transformation that we can do is we can use log transformation or we can use the square root transformation. So with the log transformation, if you have a very large value and you take the log of that, it will substantially decrease that value. But if you have a small value and you take a log, it will get a smaller but proportionally less the smaller. So the delta of all of these outlier points for coming closer to the origin is going to be more than how much these smaller values are getting closer to the origin. So that's why the distribution is going to get a little bit closer to each other. And then what we're going to see is that, okay, all of these data points, now instead of being, for example, in the 70,000 range, they are in the 12 range and they're closer to each other, right? And they're closer to also the rest of the values that that we have here. All of the data points are going to be transformed and to a smaller version of themselves. That's how the log works. But then it will fix the scale that we have. Any kind of transformation that will impact large values different than the small values and it will proportionally decrease the large values more than the small values would work here, such as a score root, right? So if you have, for example, a score root of 1600 versus a square root of 16. This is going to be, for example, 4. This is going to be 40, right? So both of them are going to get a smaller, but proportionally 1600 got way smaller than 16 got a smaller. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza X

leaf_chunk.audio: The kind of transformation that we can do is we can use log transformation or we can use the square root transformation. So with the log transformation, if you have a very large value and you take the log of that, it will substantially decrease that value. But if you have a small value and you take a log, it will get a smaller but proportionally less the smaller. So the delta of all of these outlier points for coming closer to the origin is going to be more than how much these smaller values are getting closer to the origin. So that's why the distribution is going to get a little bit closer to each other. And then what we're going to see is that, okay, all of these data points, now instead of being, for example, in the 70,000 range, they are in the 12 range and they're closer to each other, right? And they're closer to also the rest of the values that that we have here. All of the data points are going to be transformed and to a smaller version of themselves. That's how the log works. But then it will fix the scale that we have. Any kind of transformation that will impact large values different than the small values and it will proportionally decrease the large values more than the small values would work here, such as a score root, right? So if you have, for example, a score root of 1600 versus a square root of 16. This is going to be, for example, 4. This is going to be 40, right? So both of them are going to get a smaller, but proportionally 1600 got way smaller than 16 got a smaller.

children.title: Log Transformation for Non-linear Data
children.summary: Extreme values in the x-axis can distort visualizations, making data points clump together. Log transformation can help by significantly reducing the value of large numbers while minimally affecting smaller ones, thereby improving the distribution and visibility of data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's why they're going to get closer to each other. Every time that we have extreme values, any of such transformations such as log is scored with things like that would work and will help us to have a uniform kind of distribution in the x-axis or in the bi-axis. So one problem solved. Now our X dimension is log of the G and I right by the X axis is still the same. And you can see that the X-axis is still has this issue of all of these data points kind of clumped together at the higher range that we still need to fix. Okay, going into fixing that. What kind of transformation can I use so that I can push those large values more upward so that I can see the difference between them is slightly better. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Transforming Clumped Data Points
children.summary: When data points are clustered together, a transformation is needed to spread them apart. The discussion centers on identifying an appropriate transformation that serves as the opposite of square root or logarithmic functions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is the opposite problem. In the first case, I had the issue that the data points were far apart. I needed to bring them closer. Here the data points are on top of each other. I just need to push them to be farther away from each other. What kind of transformation do you think would be helpful? Make it guess. Let's see if there's anything unslide. Rest in peace. any guesses you would think that something that would be opposite of a square root or opposite of log Watch us. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Power Transformations: Effects on Data Spread
children.summary: Raising values to a power can distinguish close numbers by spreading them out, as seen with examples using powers of two and four. Small input changes yield minimal output changes at lower powers, while higher powers significantly increase the value of larger numbers, effectively addressing compression in plots with large y values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Inverse of log as the function. Power. It's the power or exponential here would work. And the reason for power is that, for example, if you have, say, Um. 10 and 11 that are kind of close to each other. And you basically try to raise each one of them to the power of two. For example, this is not a good transformation. And we're not talking about this range. We're talking about the range of values being in 200. Here we're going to get 100. Here we're going to get 121. So you're seeing that with the small delta in the input, if we take, if you raise it to a certain power, they're going to be distinguishable. And that's exactly what we want. So here, but if I go crazy and I raise it to the power of four, right? So you can see that now all of those data points are going to be more spreaded out. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: Inverse of log as the function. Power. It's the power or exponential here would work. And the reason for power is that, for example, if you have, say, Um. 10 and 11 that are kind of close to each other. And you basically try to raise each one of them to the power of two. For example, this is not a good transformation. And we're not talking about this range. We're talking about the range of values being in 200. Here we're going to get 100. Here we're going to get 121. So you're seeing that with the small delta in the input, if we take, if you raise it to a certain power, they're going to be distinguishable. And that's exactly what we want. So here, but if I go crazy and I raise it to the power of four, right? So you can see that now all of those data points are going to be more spreaded out.

children.title: Data Transformation for Non-linear Visualization and Regression
children.summary: Log transformation reduces the x-axis range and mitigates outliers, while raising the y-axis to the fourth power addresses large value clustering, enhancing visualization. A linear regression can model the relationship between the logarithm of X and the fourth power of Y, revealing semi-linear trends. Proper data transformation is crucial for effective linear regression, ensuring features are comparable and uncovering meaningful insights from the original data.

children.title: Transformations for Non-linear Data Visualization
children.summary: Log transformation was applied to reduce the x-axis range and mitigate outliers, while the y-axis was raised to the power of 4 to address the clustering of large y values. Power transformations significantly increase large values while minimally affecting smaller ones, aiding in better data visualization.

children.title: Linear Regression with Data Transformation
children.summary: A linear regression line can be applied to model the relationship between the logarithm of the X variable and the fourth power of the Y variable, revealing a semi-linear trend. The equation is structured as y = m * log(x) + b, allowing for a transformation that facilitates understanding non-linear associations through linear regression. This approach demonstrates the importance of data transformation in uncovering relationships not immediately apparent from initial plots.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But if I do that, essentially what you're seeing is that now my data the plants are spread a little bit more. And now what we're seeing is a semi-linear trend as opposed to that non-linear trend that we had seen before. So what we can do with this is now put a linear regression line. Now we can understand the relationship between G and I and adulculatory serrate using a linear regression model. What do I mean by that? So if I have a linear regression line from data aid, you can see that I have X dimension, I have Y dimension, it just happens that my X dimension is now log of X. And then I have Y dimension, that my Y dimension just happens to be now. y to the power of 4 or that variable to the power of 4, which essentially will help me to use the linear regression line based on the template that we have that was y equal to m times x plus b. So x right now is log of x. y right now is y to the power of 4. So now I have a linear regression line that models the relationship between log of x and y to the power of 4. which is pretty neat. We couldn't do it or we couldn't have guessed this kind of association by just looking at the plot. That's why we needed to do transformation and that's why it's pretty important. So what we can do afterwards is to just restructure this equation and just write the equation as y equal to the transform version of the right hand side. So I can say that, okay, m times log of x plus b to the power of 1 over 4 is the equation that you're looking for, which I can go ahead and plot that. We would have never guessed that this is the relationship between between these two variables, but now using linear regression, we fit it in non-linear function or association between the x dimension and the y dimension. leaf_chunk.audio: But if I do that, essentially what you're seeing is that now my data the plants are spread a little bit more. And now what we're seeing is a semi-linear trend as opposed to that non-linear trend that we had seen before. So what we can do with this is now put a linear regression line. Now we can understand the relationship between G and I and adulculatory serrate using a linear regression model. What do I mean by that? So if I have a linear regression line from data aid, you can see that I have X dimension, I have Y dimension, it just happens that my X dimension is now log of X. And then I have Y dimension, that my Y dimension just happens to be now. y to the power of 4 or that variable to the power of 4, which essentially will help me to use the linear regression line based on the template that we have that was y equal to m times x plus b. So x right now is log of x. y right now is y to the power of 4. So now I have a linear regression line that models the relationship between log of x and y to the power of 4. which is pretty neat. We couldn't do it or we couldn't have guessed this kind of association by just looking at the plot. That's why we needed to do transformation and that's why it's pretty important. So what we can do afterwards is to just restructure this equation and just write the equation as y equal to the transform version of the right hand side. So I can say that, okay, m times log of x plus b to the power of 1 over 4 is the equation that you're looking for, which I can go ahead and plot that. We would have never guessed that this is the relationship between between these two variables, but now using linear regression, we fit it in non-linear function or association between the x dimension and the y dimension. Now you see that I'm living in the xy dimension, because I can because I have the equation for it.

children.title: Importance of Data Transformation in Linear Regression
children.summary: Effective linear regression involves more than just applying the method; it requires proper data preparation and transformation to ensure features are on a similar scale and in a linear space. This enhances the modeling power and allows for meaningful insights from the original data. The discussion emphasizes the practice of addressing issues through transformations, illustrating how to achieve a linear relationship between variables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's pretty neat. This is what I want you to take away from this lecture that when we teach you linear regression, we teach you way more than linear regression, we teach you techniques that if you do... If you prepare your data data well enough and you do the transformation good enough and you have all of the features in kind of linear space and also the same scale from each other. The modeling that you will do is going to be very more powerful if you transform it back to the original kind of space of your data. And this is the kind of trend that we will be able to extract using a simple linear regression. Mother. Okay, you might tell me that Nargis, this was difficult to just guess what issues we have and how to fix those issues. And it was kind of a guessing game that you use while you're to the power for a lot of X for fixing each one of those issues. But I think that was a good practice for you to see how I arrived at each one of those transformations that I did. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza X

children.title: Tukey-Mosteller Bulge Diagram and Data Visualization
children.summary: The Tukey-Mosteller Bulge Diagram helps identify necessary transformations for achieving linearity in data by analyzing bulges in the data. Various transformations like log, square root, and power are discussed, emphasizing the importance of understanding their application rather than rote memorization. Visualization theory is introduced, highlighting the use of information channels to enhance plot clarity, with examples illustrating the need for effective visualizations, such as using histograms or KDEs instead of scatter plots for better data interpretation.

children.title: Tukey-Mosteller Bulge Diagram: Data Transformation
children.summary: The Tukey-Mosteller Bulge Diagram serves as a reference for identifying appropriate transformations to achieve linearity in data. By analyzing how data bulges, one can determine whether to transform the X-axis, Y-axis, or both. The discussion emphasizes that while multiple transformations may be available, understanding the underlying logic is crucial for selecting the most effective solution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: For future, what you can do is you can start to reason in your mind in the same way that I started reasoning about the plot that we'd see, right? But additionally, you can use this chart. This is called Tukimasa or Balch diagram as kind of your reference guide. Essentially what you would do is you would take a look at where, how your data bulges. And if your data bulges in a certain way, you would take a look at the transformation and the x-axis and the y-axis and those are the transformations that you could use. Sometimes only a single transformation might solve your problem, so you might only want to transform X, or you might only want to transform Y. In some cases, you might want to transform both in order to get the result that you want. So kind of to put it in practice, the trend that we saw in our data was that our data was bulging in the direction that we're seeing in this top left corner. And what we did was we basically kind of guess what kind of transformation I need on the X axis, what kind of transformation I need. I need a new y-axis and we're going to head and transform the data. Again, I did both of the transformation. You might not need both. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: For future, what you can do is you can start to reason in your mind in the same way that I started reasoning about the plot that we'd see, right? But additionally, you can use this chart. This is called Tukimasa or Balch diagram as kind of your reference guide. Essentially what you would do is you would take a look at where, how your data bulges. And if your data bulges in a certain way, you would take a look at the transformation and the x-axis and the y-axis and those are the transformations that you could use. Sometimes only a single transformation might solve your problem, so you might only want to transform X, or you might only want to transform Y. In some cases, you might want to transform both in order to get the result that you want. So kind of to put it in practice, the trend that we saw in our data was that our data was bulging in the direction that we're seeing in this top left corner. And what we did was we basically kind of guess what kind of transformation I need on the X axis, what kind of transformation I need. I need a new y-axis and we're going to head and transform the data. Again, I did both of the transformation. You might not need both.

children.title: Transformations for Data Visualization
children.summary: Discusses the use of various transformations such as log, square root, and power transformations to address data bulging in visualization. Emphasizes understanding the reasoning behind these transformations rather than memorizing specific plots, which will be provided in a reference sheet. Highlights the importance of evaluating data readiness for modeling versus the need for further data preparation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You might only need one depending on the shape of your data and depending on how deep it bulges into one direction. Remember that I was telling you that, okay, we do a log of X transformation. We could have also transformed using a square root of X. So that's kind of the reason that we could have done that. Or basically we did want to the power of four transformation or any power transformation would have worked in those skates. So, why to the power of two? Why to the power of three? All of that would have also worked. So this is a nice kind of reference. You don't need to memorize this plot. This bulge diagram, we're going to give it to you in your reference sheet. But I'm expecting you that after I walk you through all of the steps of the reasoning, you should be able to reason it again before using this plot. How are we doing so far? I think we're done with the difficult part of the lecture. Good sideways, not so much. Good. It kind of makes sense. All of these visualization topics that we're covering is kind of really striking the balance between evaluating whether the data is ready for modeling or we need to do more data preparation, data processing or if you're ready to move forward to the um, to the modeling phase. Remember that I was telling you that, okay, we do a log of X transformation. We could have also transformed using a square root of X. So that's kind of the reason that we could have done that. Or basically we did want to the power of four transformation or any power transformation would have worked in those skates. So, why to the power of two? Why to the power of three? All of that would have also worked. So this is a nice kind of reference. You don't need to memorize this plot. This bulge diagram, we're going to give it to you in your reference sheet. But I'm expecting you that after I walk you through all of the steps of the reasoning, you should be able to reason it again before using this plot. How are we doing so far? I think we're done with the difficult part of the lecture. Good sideways, not so much. Good. It kind of makes sense. children.title: Introduction to Visualization Theory
children.summary: Visualization theory focuses on the considerations necessary for creating effective plots. Key aspects include utilizing different information channels, such as X/Y axes, color, markings, conditioning, and context, to enhance the clarity and impact of visual data representations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So now I want to switch gear and talk about visualization theory. Um, this is kind of going to be a little bit of... a little bit eye opening in a sense that you will see different considerations that you should have when you're creating different plots. children.title: Effective Data Visualization for Human Interpretation
children.summary: The importance of creating meaningful plots for human understanding is emphasized, using the example of Titanic ticket prices plotted against age. It is noted that the scatter plot fails to convey clear information due to overplotting, particularly in the lower price range. A histogram or KDE is suggested as more effective alternatives for visualizing this data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Why are we talking about information channel because we need to make sure that the plots that you're creating that you're creating actually for humans are meaningful. So for example, here what I plotted is the amount of the amount that people paid for, the ticket for Titanic and depending on their age. And if you're taking a look at what people are going to see in this plot, people are not going to be able to gather much information from it, because majority of your data is kind of overplotted in the bottom area here. So you can make that okay, how much a 20 year old person is gonna pay versus a 60 year old necessarily because the data is just spread it out so in the zero to hundred dollar range. leaf_chunk.audio: Why are we talking about information channel because we need to make sure that the plots that you're creating that you're creating actually for humans are meaningful. So for example, here what I plotted is the amount of the amount that people paid for, the ticket for Titanic and depending on their age. And if you're taking a look at what people are going to see in this plot, people are not going to be able to gather much information from it, because majority of your data is kind of overplotted in the bottom area here. So you can make that okay, how much a 20 year old person is gonna pay versus a 60 year old necessarily because the data is just spread it out so in the zero to hundred dollar range.

children.title: Importance of Visualization in Data Analysis
children.summary: Effective use of information channels enhances understanding of data, with visualization serving as a crucial complement to summary statistics. Even when different datasets share identical summary statistics like mean and standard deviation, their visual representations can reveal significant differences, emphasizing the expressive power of visualizations over mere statistics.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what we need to make sure is that we use information channels more effectively. And what we're gonna do is that we will also try to understand why visualization in the first place is helpful. So What do you what I plot it here is a set of A set of different data points that have the same summary statistic. So you're seeing that the mean of the x-axis, the mean of the y-axis, standard deviations, all of that is the same. But when you visualize data sets, they're very different from each other. So that's why we think about summary statistic and visualization always being like really good complementary things that would go together. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: So what we need to make sure is that we use information channels more effectively. And what we're gonna do is that we will also try to understand why visualization in the first place is helpful. So What do you what I plot it here is a set of A set of different data points that have the same summary statistic. So you're seeing that the mean of the x-axis, the mean of the y-axis, standard deviations, all of that is the same. But when you visualize data sets, they're very different from each other. So that's why we think about summary statistic and visualization always being like really good complementary things that would go together.

children.title: Model Evaluation: Metrics and Visualization Techniques
children.summary: Model performance evaluation involves reporting accuracy metrics and using visualization techniques to gain insights. For instance, analyzing Titanic passenger ages can be enhanced through various plotting methods, including histograms, KDE, and rock plots.

children.title: Model Evaluation: Metrics and Visualization Techniques
children.summary: To evaluate model performance, report accuracy metrics and plot data distributions for comprehensive insights. For example, analyzing Titanic passenger ages can utilize various plotting methods such as histograms, KDE, or rock plots to visualize the data effectively.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Every time that we want you to report the accuracy of your model or the output of your model, we would tell you that, okay, give us the metric or the value for the metric of the evaluation, as well as plot the distribution so that we can kind of see how your model is performing for different range of the data, kind of to make sure that we capture all the information that we can get about your model performance. So mean by information channel? So let's take a look at this exercise. So if I take a look at the age of people who were on Titanic, right? And I have one serious object that has all of those age values. There are different ways that I can plot it. There is a way that I can directly use histogram, we have seen it before, I can also use KDE, or I can use rock plot. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Rock blood visualization and marker encoding
children.summary: Rock blood visualization uses age distribution markers to indicate individual ages at data points, with age represented on the Y-axis and potential overplotting issues. The choice of marker type impacts data clarity, with 2D markers preferred for illustrating relationships, such as age and fear, in scatter plots. In 2D visualization, multiple channels like X and Y coordinates, color, and area can represent more than three variables.

children.title: Rock blood visualization: age distribution markers
children.summary: Rock blood is a visualization technique that uses markers to indicate individual ages at each data point. The Y-axis represents the age distribution, with horizontal lines marking each individual's age, though this method can lead to overplotting. The process involves encoding the age data into pixel positions on the plot, illustrating how markers are placed based on specific age values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is called the rock blood. The way that you can call rock blood is just SMS.rock blood. It's not going to be a very fun blood, but what it does is basically that at every given data point, it will put a marker. So essentially, what you're seeing here is that it is telling me that the age of the individual, there are some people who were like infants and we have indicators for them. We have, for example, a person that looks like that they were kind of in 72 year old and we basically tried to have an indicator for each month of them. As you can see for trying to plot this distribution or look at the age distribution of individuals, there will be a lot of overplodding, right? So this is not going to be a really good visualization, but just so you know that we used exactly one channel in this exact example, that that channel is basically the Y axis. So I'm using the Y access in order to plot this information. So what I can do next is I can go ahead and say and show you how we're creating the plot using the channels that we have. So I have the data frame or I have that column specific leap. I would say that I'm using a marker for each one of these individual rows. So I'm saying that the marker that I'm going to use for the rows is just this horizontal line. And then I'm using a specific encoding. for the channel that I want to use. What is the channel? The channel is the y-axis. So I would say that, okay, for this is specific age of 22, put it in the pixel 10 position from the bottom. So it will go ahead and put not pixel 10. Pixel 10 would be somewhere here on 22. And it will basically take one marker, encoded into location, and then that's how the rot plot is actually created. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: This is called the rock blood. The way that you can call rock blood is just SMS.rock blood. It's not going to be a very fun blood, but what it does is basically that at every given data point, it will put a marker. So essentially, what you're seeing here is that it is telling me that the age of the individual, there are some people who were like infants and we have indicators for them. We have, for example, a person that looks like that they were kind of in 72 year old and we basically tried to have an indicator for each month of them. As you can see for trying to plot this distribution or look at the age distribution of individuals, there will be a lot of overplodding, right? So this is not going to be a really good visualization, but just so you know that we used exactly one channel in this exact example, that that channel is basically the Y axis. So I'm using the Y access in order to plot this information. So what I can do next is I can go ahead and say and show you how we're creating the plot using the channels that we have. So I have the data frame or I have that column specific leap. I would say that I'm using a marker for each one of these individual rows. So I'm saying that the marker that I'm going to use for the rows is just this horizontal line. And then I'm using a specific encoding. for the channel that I want to use. What is the channel? The channel is the y-axis. So I would say that, okay, for this is specific age of 22, put it in the pixel 10 position from the bottom. So it will go ahead and put not pixel 10. Pixel 10 would be somewhere here on 22. And it will basically take one marker, encoded into location, and then that's how the rot plot is actually created.

children.title: Marker Choice in 1D vs 2D Encoding
children.summary: Changing the marker type affects data visualization; for instance, using circles instead of a specific marker can obscure differences in a 1D plot. The importance of selecting appropriate markers is highlighted, as a 2D marker would be more suitable for visualizing relationships, such as between age and fear, in a scatter plot.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We could go ahead and change how we're using the marker or the encoding. For example, if I change the marker to be like these circles, what I can create is what you're seeing on the right hand side. So the position is still the same. The encoding is the same, but the marker is different. So you can see that the choice of marker here is also important, because I was able to tell the differences between different ages using the normal rock plot, but with the marker being different, now I can't basically see anything because I'm plotting it, I'm using it to the marker in a 1D encoding, if that makes sense. Right, because it basically grabs the y-axis, x-axis, and also it has the width, it grabs also the y-axis. So it has two dimensions in order to encode only one dimension information that we have. So the more appropriate place to use this specific 2D marker for us would have been a 2D plot or a plot that has a 2-dimensional encoding. So a two dimensional encoding could be that, okay, I want to do a scatter plot. I want to take a look at the association between age and fear. So in that case, a 2D marker would have been helpful because I can say that, okay, I'm gonna use these two dimensions of the age being 22 and the fair being 7.25. leaf_chunk.audio: We could go ahead and change how we're using the marker or the encoding. For example, if I change the marker to be like these circles, what I can create is what you're seeing on the right hand side. So the position is still the same. The encoding is the same, but the marker is different. So you can see that the choice of marker here is also important, because I was able to tell the differences between different ages using the normal rock plot, but with the marker being different, now I can't basically see anything because I'm plotting it, I'm using it to the marker in a 1D encoding, if that makes sense. Right, because it basically grabs the y-axis, x-axis, and also it has the width, it grabs also the y-axis. So it has two dimensions in order to encode only one dimension information that we have. So the more appropriate place to use this specific 2D marker for us would have been a 2D plot or a plot that has a 2-dimensional encoding. So a two dimensional encoding could be that, okay, I want to do a scatter plot. I want to take a look at the association between age and fear. So in that case, a 2D marker would have been helpful because I can say that, okay, I'm gonna use these two dimensions of the age being 22 and the fair being 7.25.

children.title: Encoding Variables in 2D Visualization
children.summary: In a 2D channel space, multiple information channels can be utilized for encoding variables. In the discussed example, four channels are identified: X and Y coordinates, color, and area of circles, allowing for the representation of more than three variables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And I'm gonna encode it in two dimensional space as opposed to one dimensional space. And in that case, it will make sense for me to have a 2D marker in a 2D channel space. So this is what we're going to start talking about and thinking about about how many information channels are being utilized and how to effectively use all the information channels that we have. So for example, in this example, we encoded more than three variables. But what I want to ask you is that how many variables are being coding here exactly? So the question was how many channels are being used or how many variables are being recording in this plot. Looks like Ford is a winner. Can someone explain what those Ford channels are? Yes. X and Y 2. We have the color tree. We have the area of each of those circles that we have. So we have four different information channels that we're utilizing. leaf_chunk.audio: And I'm gonna encode it in two dimensional space as opposed to one dimensional space. And in that case, it will make sense for me to have a 2D marker in a 2D channel space. children.title: Information Channels and Effective Data Visualization Techniques
children.summary: The discussion covers the strategic use of information channels and variable encoding to accurately represent multiple variables, highlighting the dangers of improper length encoding in bar charts. Effective plotting techniques include using a single scale for accurate comparisons, employing percentages for trend visualization, and considering color and context. Examples include comparing car quality between Sweden and the USA, and utilizing truncation in COVID case data visualizations to emphasize significant trends.

children.title: Information Channels and Encoding Misuse
children.summary: The discussion emphasizes the strategic use of information channels and variable encoding to effectively represent multiple variables. An example of improper length encoding in a bar chart highlights how misleading visualizations can misrepresent data, underscoring the necessity for appropriate techniques. A break is announced before proceeding with best practices for encoding and visualization.

children.title: Information Channels and Encoding Misuse
children.summary: The discussion covers the strategic use of information channels and variable encoding, emphasizing the importance of effectively utilizing different channels to represent multiple variables. An example of improper length encoding in a bar chart illustrates how misleading visualizations can misrepresent data, stressing the need for appropriate techniques. A break is announced before continuing with best practices for encoding and visualization.

children.title: Information Channels and Variable Encoding
children.summary: The discussion focuses on strategizing the use of various information channels and determining their priority based on the variables being created. A plot will illustrate poor utilization of axes, and the segment emphasizes the importance of effectively using different channels and encodings to represent multiple variables, highlighting that there are countless possibilities for encoding beyond just basic variables.

children.title: Abusing Length Encoding in Visualizations
children.summary: The example illustrates the misuse of length encoding on the x-axis in a bar chart comparing car nationalities, where the length of the bars misleadingly suggests an order or superiority among the cars. This misrepresentation fails to convey accurate information about the data points and highlights the importance of using appropriate visualization techniques.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So in this example, what you're seeing is that the x-axis or the length same formation of the x-axis is being abused. In a sense that when we're trying to plot the association of different cars to different nationalities or to different countries, what we're seeing here is that if USA is the first, that means that there is something less about a car because the length of the bar is smaller as opposed to basically having a scatter point for all of these dimensions that we have or any other kind of plots that we could use. Because the length of these bars here does not have does not. In code any information and the ordering of all of the data points that we have here and also the data points that we have here does not include any information. So that's why this is an example that the lengths. Encoding is being abused in this example on the x axis. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Effective Plotting and Data Visualization Techniques
children.summary: Length in visualizations can misrepresent data types, illustrated by a case study comparing car quality in Sweden and the USA. Key considerations include scale, color, and context, with a single scale necessary for accurate comparisons, such as cancer screenings and abortion rates. Percentages can show trends effectively, and side-by-side comparisons enhance clarity. For COVID case data, truncating axes can highlight significant spikes, while maximizing plot space improves clarity; color choice is crucial for data perception, with tools available for effective usage.

children.title: Effective Plotting and Data Visualization Techniques
children.summary: Length in visualizations can misrepresent data types, as shown in a case study comparing car quality between Sweden and the USA. Key plotting considerations include the use of scale, color, and context; using a single scale for cancer screenings and abortion rates is essential to avoid misleading interpretations. Percentages can effectively illustrate trends, and side-by-side comparisons enhance clarity. Additionally, when visualizing COVID case data, truncating axes highlights significant spikes while maximizing plot space improves clarity. Color significantly impacts data perception, with tools available to assist in effective color usage.

children.title: Effective Plotting Considerations and Case Study
children.summary: Length in visualizations can misrepresent data types and does not quantify differences, as illustrated by comparing car quality between Sweden and the USA. Key considerations for creating plots include efficient use of scale, color, and context. A case study on Planned Parenthood's services from 2006 to 2013 highlights the relationship between cancer screenings and abortion rates.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: All right, restarting in 5. 4, 3, 2, 1. There's a question in this slide about why the length was abused here. It's basically because the length encodes the type and the length does not measure anything quantitative between different categories or different countries. So that's why you might think that a car from Sweden is much better than a car in USA. If you're thinking about lengths or these bars measuring any quantitative value. So again, what I said was we're going to take a look at different considerations when you're creating plots. So we're going to go and talk about how we can use this scale efficiently, how we can use the color efficiently, marking, conditioning, and how we can also consider the context when you're creating different plots. So let's dive in with the xy or the scale thing. So this is a case study from When there was a hearing about plant parenthood being accused of selling a boarded fetal tissue. So the idea was that they were saying that the number of cancer screenings went down and the number of abortions went up from 2006 up until 2013. And the idea was that, okay, let's investigate and let's have a hearing on what's happening with the practice of plant parenthood. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza >

# Some data cleaning to help with the next example
df = pd.DataFrame(index=wb.index)
df['lit'] = wb['Adult literacy rate: Female: % ages 15 and older: 2005-14'] \
+ wb["Adult literacy rate: Male: % ages 15 and older: 2005-14"]
df['inc' ] = wb['gni']
df.dropna(inplace=True)
plt.scatter(df["inc"], df[ "lit"])
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Adult literacy rate");
What is making this plot non-linear?
. There are a few extremely large values for gross national income that are distorting the horizontal scale of the plot. If we rescaled the x-values
such that these large values become proportionally smaller, the plot would be more linear
. There are too many large values of adult literacy rate all clumped together at the top of the plot. If we rescaled the y-axis such that large values of
y are more spread out, the plot would be more linear

df['inc' ] = wb['gni']
df.dropna(inplace=True)
plt.scatter(df["inc"], df[ "lit"])
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Adult literacy rate");
What is making this plot non-linear?
. There are a few extremely large values for gross national income that are distorting the horizontal scale of the plot. If we rescaled the x-values
such that these large values become proportionally smaller, the plot would be more linear
. There are too many large values of adult literacy rate all clumped together at the top of the plot. If we rescaled the y-axis such that large values of
y are more spread out, the plot would be more linear
First, we can transform the x-values such that very large values of x become smaller. This can be achieved by performing a log transformation of the
gross national income data. When we take the logarithm of a large number, this number becomes proportionally much smaller relative to its original
value. When we take the log of a small number, the number does not change very significantly relative to its starting value.

leaf_chunk.audio: All right, restarting in 5. 4, 3, 2, 1. There's a question in this slide about why the length was abused here. It's basically because the length encodes the type and the length does not measure anything quantitative between different categories or different countries. So that's why you might think that a car from Sweden is much better than a car in USA. If you're thinking about lengths or these bars measuring any quantitative value. So again, what I said was we're going to take a look at different considerations when you're creating plots. So we're going to go and talk about how we can use this scale efficiently, how we can use the color efficiently, marking, conditioning, and how we can also consider the context when you're creating different plots. So let's dive in with the xy or the scale thing. So this is a case study from When there was a hearing about plant parenthood being accused of selling a boarded fetal tissue. So the idea was that they were saying that the number of cancer screenings went down and the number of abortions went up from 2006 up until 2013. And the idea was that, okay, let's investigate and let's have a hearing on what's happening with the practice of plant parenthood.

children.title: Data Visualization: Scale and Misleading Information
children.summary: The issue with plotting data lies in the differing scales used for cancer screenings and abortion numbers, which can create misleading interpretations. To accurately compare these metrics, it's essential to use a single scale, preferably percentages or total cases, allowing for a clear visual representation of the relationship between the two variables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But there is an issue with the way that information is plotted and thus figured. and you might have guessed what the issue is. And the issue essentially is that why access to scale and the why access changes for different lines. In a sense that here we're plotting 2 million, the value of 2 million and the cancerous screening is starting at the top and then going down to almost a million. But when you're looking at the other plots, so you're seeing that the abortion numbers are starting from upper 2000 number and then to lower 300,000 cases. But you're seeing that almost the endpoint of the number of abortions and this starting point of the cancer looks to be at the same place that you might think that one went down and one exactly went up and that's how they're going to be compromised. That's how cancer screening and abortions are basically trying to be equal. the total across one specific year is going to be equal. That's why the decrease in one is equal to the increase in the other one. But the issue is that they're on different scales. The better way for us to visualize this data so that it's not kind of communicating, misleading information, because at first sight you might have that misleading information is to put one scale. This is very, very important. So there is one scale, specifically, that that better data scale is just the percentage of the cases or the total number of cases. So you basically compare magnitudes on the same scale exactly. And you don't have like an imaginary scale for one line, an imaginary scale for another line and just compare and contrast them. So using the top one, you can see that, okay, the cancer screening went down for whatever reason and abortion went up, but still proportionally there is way more cancer screenings than abortion cases. leaf_chunk.audio: But there is an issue with the way that information is plotted and thus figured. and you might have guessed what the issue is. And the issue essentially is that why access to scale and the why access changes for different lines. In a sense that here we're plotting 2 million, the value of 2 million and the cancerous screening is starting at the top and then going down to almost a million. But when you're looking at the other plots, so you're seeing that the abortion numbers are starting from upper 2000 number and then to lower 300,000 cases. But you're seeing that almost the endpoint of the number of abortions and this starting point of the cancer looks to be at the same place that you might think that one went down and one exactly went up and that's how they're going to be compromised. That's how cancer screening and abortions are basically trying to be equal. the total across one specific year is going to be equal. That's why the decrease in one is equal to the increase in the other one. But the issue is that they're on different scales. The better way for us to visualize this data so that it's not kind of communicating, misleading information, because at first sight you might have that misleading information is to put one scale. This is very, very important. So there is one scale, specifically, that that better data scale is just the percentage of the cases or the total number of cases. So you basically compare magnitudes on the same scale exactly. And you don't have like an imaginary scale for one line, an imaginary scale for another line and just compare and contrast them. So using the top one, you can see that, okay, the cancer screening went down for whatever reason and abortion went up, but still proportionally there is way more cancer screenings than abortion cases.

children.title: Comparing Percentages and Visualizing Data
children.summary: Percentages can effectively illustrate trends, such as the decline in cancer screenings and the increase in abortion cases. A side-by-side color-coded comparison for specific years, like 2006 and 2013, allows for clearer visualization of these changes while maintaining consistent scales. It's crucial to ensure that scales are appropriately shared and not manipulated to misrepresent data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You could also do percentages. So this is an example that the percentage is also shown. So you can kind of see that cancer is screening when down proportionally. And then you can see that abortion cases went up and then you can kind of compare the percentages with each other. And then looking at those percentages, again, is going to give you some information about one is increasing and one is decreasing and so on and so forth. So the better a better way to visualize exactly the same information is to just compare exactly what you want to, the things that you want to compare. What you want to compare is exactly the years. What happens in year 2006 and what happens in the year 2013. And what you want is to have different colors for them and have side-by-side comparisons between them. Again, the Y-axis is still the same. The scale is the same between them. But again, you can look at the category of abortion, the category of cancer. You would see that there is a decline. You would see that there is an increase. But also, you would see that proportionally, there is way more cancer is screen than abortion cases. So this is kind of seeing all that information in one place. So this is kind of making sure that the scales are the same. The scales are shared. The scales are not abused in the case of car nationalities that we had seen before. And that's a very important information. Also speaking of the scale, you don't necessarily need to show and reveal the entire data. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: You could also do percentages. So this is an example that the percentage is also shown. So you can kind of see that cancer is screening when down proportionally. And then you can see that abortion cases went up and then you can kind of compare the percentages with each other. And then looking at those percentages, again, is going to give you some information about one is increasing and one is decreasing and so on and so forth. So the better a better way to visualize exactly the same information is to just compare exactly what you want to, the things that you want to compare. What you want to compare is exactly the years. What happens in year 2006 and what happens in the year 2013. And what you want is to have different colors for them and have side-by-side comparisons between them. Again, the Y-axis is still the same. The scale is the same between them. But again, you can look at the category of abortion, the category of cancer. You would see that there is a decline. You would see that there is an increase. But also, you would see that proportionally, there is way more cancer is screen than abortion cases. So this is kind of seeing all that information in one place. So this is kind of making sure that the scales are the same. The scales are shared. children.title: Data Visualization: Focusing on Key Spikes
children.summary: The segment discusses how to effectively visualize COVID case data in Australia from March 2020 to August 2021, emphasizing the importance of truncating axes to highlight significant spikes. It explains that for detailed analysis of specific peaks, it's better to limit the data shown, while for general trends, the entire dataset should be included. Additionally, maximizing the use of plot space and selectively omitting data points below a certain threshold can enhance clarity and resolution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is an example of I think the COVID cases in Australia and what you're seeing that it starts from March 4 or sometime in March 2020 and then it goes all the way to August of 2021. And the data is very spreaded out. You would see those as small spikes here and there, but then you would see that largest spike happening around meat March, right? If you want to explain what's happening when you had a very kind of high peak, you'd really want to make sure that you only show the area that is of interest. It doesn't, you don't need to show the entire data, but truncate your data or truncate the x-axis or y-axis to focus on exactly what you're interested in. So this is also another important information that the right hand side plot is much better than the left hand side plot if your interests or your use case is to study this spike. If your interest is just to look at the trend generally, yes, should the entire data and use that entire data. What you need to also make sure is that you use the entire kind of canvas of the plot as much as possible. Why do I say it? Because what you're seeing is that The markings here are far apart from each other so each one of them is 400,000 data points or data points apart from Is that so that's 40,000 40,000 data points apart from each other right if you want to have a more resolution what you could do is you could basically use up this extra Wife space that we have and zoom in to only look at the differences in the area of the plot or your line that you're interested in so that's a very important information. The other important information that is done properly on this plot is that between 0 and 20 or 2.1K, the data is not shown. Which is reasonable you don't need to always start from the origin you can basically troncate and say that I'm starting from this minimum value that is on my plot but then go all the way to kind of around the area of the maximum value in your plot so that you get the maximum resolution for the area that you have the changes in your plot so that's something else to consider. leaf_chunk.audio: So this is an example of I think the COVID cases in Australia and what you're seeing that it starts from March 4 or sometime in March 2020 and then it goes all the way to August of 2021. And the data is very spreaded out. You would see those as small spikes here and there, but then you would see that largest spike happening around meat March, right? If you want to explain what's happening when you had a very kind of high peak, you'd really want to make sure that you only show the area that is of interest. It doesn't, you don't need to show the entire data, but truncate your data or truncate the x-axis or y-axis to focus on exactly what you're interested in. So this is also another important information that the right hand side plot is much better than the left hand side plot if your interests or your use case is to study this spike. If your interest is just to look at the trend generally, yes, should the entire data and use that entire data. What you need to also make sure is that you use the entire kind of canvas of the plot as much as possible. Why do I say it? Because what you're seeing is that The markings here are far apart from each other so each one of them is 400,000 data points or data points apart from Is that so that's 40,000 40,000 data points apart from each other right if you want to have a more resolution what you could do is you could basically use up this extra Wife space that we have and zoom in to only look at the differences in the area of the plot or your line that you're interested in so that's a very important information. The other important information that is done properly on this plot is that between 0 and 20 or 2.1K, the data is not shown. Which is reasonable you don't need to always start from the origin you can basically troncate and say that I'm starting from this minimum value that is on my plot but then go all the way to kind of around the area of the maximum value in your plot so that you get the maximum resolution for the area that you have the changes in your plot so that's something else to consider.

children.title: Importance of Color in Data Visualization
children.summary: Color plays a crucial role in data visualization, affecting how information is perceived. Tools like Color Oracle can simulate how individuals with different types of color blindness view plots, ensuring that visual representations are accessible to all students. This practice is essential for creating inclusive and effective presentations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Thinking about the color. The color is really important. And I'm going to talk a little bit about why it's important and what's the science behind choosing different colors. So this is an example that for people who are not red-green blind and they have full vision, they would see a number inside this figure. But if you go ahead and simulate it, let me see. If I can get the simulation going. Good. So if we go ahead and simulate that you can also simulate it using different kind of software that you have, but what I'm using is color oracle, which basically it will simulate what people with different kinds of color blindness would experience if they look at the plot that you created or they look at the slides that you create. A lot of time when we create a slides, we also simulate it to make sure that all the figures are visible by all the students as well. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: Thinking about the color. The color is really important. And I'm going to talk a little bit about why it's important and what's the science behind choosing different colors. So this is an example that for people who are not red-green blind and they have full vision, they would see a number inside this figure. But if you go ahead and simulate it, let me see. If I can get the simulation going. Good. So if we go ahead and simulate that you can also simulate it using different kind of software that you have, but what I'm using is color oracle, which basically it will simulate what people with different kinds of color blindness would experience if they look at the plot that you created or they look at the slides that you create. A lot of time when we create a slides, we also simulate it to make sure that all the figures are visible by all the students as well.

children.title: Color Maps and Human Perception in Visualization
children.summary: The choice of color in visualizations affects accessibility, particularly for individuals with color deficiencies; perceptually uniform color maps like the Veritas Color Map improve data communication. Human perception is limited, as shown by difficulties in estimating area versus length; pie charts are ineffective for angle interpretation, while scatter plots are clearer. Bar charts should avoid baseline manipulation, and line charts are ideal for comparing income differences, highlighting the need for context, clear labeling, and effective visual channels.

children.title: Color Maps: Accessibility and Data Representation
children.summary: The choice of color in visualizations significantly impacts accessibility for individuals with color deficiencies. The jet color map can mislead viewers in X-ray imaging due to distracting visual spikes, while perceptually uniform color maps like the Veritas Color Map enhance data communication. Color schemes can differentiate qualitative and quantitative variables, using qualitative for categories and sequential/divergent for magnitude, allowing for clear representation of opposing views.

children.title: Color Maps and Accessibility in Visualization
children.summary: The choice of color in visualizations affects accessibility, especially for those with color deficiencies. Different color maps convey intensities and values differently, as shown by comparing the jet color map with another map that emphasizes central areas in red and yellow, while less active regions are represented in blue.

children.title: Color Maps and Accessibility in Visualization
children.summary: The effectiveness of a plot's color choices significantly impacts its accessibility, particularly for individuals with color deficiencies. Different color maps encode various intensities and values, as demonstrated by comparing the jet color map and another map, highlighting central areas in red and yellow while less active regions appear in blue.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example, if I go ahead and simulate this view, You could kind of guess that there is some number in the center but it's really hard to see. And again, you can simulate for different types of color deficiencies. And then you would basically be able to see if your plot is accessible or not. So speaking of accessibility, what makes a plot accessible or useful or choice of the color More effective than other kind of color choices is based on the color maps that we're choosing So each kind of color map has a set of different colors and different colors are going to encode different intensities or different values on your plot So for example here The same concept or same set of pixel values are shown or visualized by jet color map and also wear this color map. So you can see that there are like central pieces that are highlighted using does. Here it would be. the red area and here it would be the yellow area and there are other areas that are less active around the corner and those are all the blue areas that you see in both color maps. children.title: Color Maps in X-ray Visualization and Data Representation
children.summary: The jet color map can mislead viewers in X-ray imaging by creating distracting visual spikes, particularly for general viewers who may struggle with prominent colors. Perceptually uniform color maps, such as the Veritas Color Map, improve data communication by maintaining consistent lightness and aiding color-blind users. Color can differentiate qualitative and quantitative variables, with qualitative schemes for categories and sequential/divergent schemes for magnitude, while divergent colormaps allow for distinct representation of opposing views without sequential encoding.

children.title: Issues with Jet Color Map in X-ray Visualization
children.summary: The jet color map can mislead viewers by creating distracting visual spikes, particularly in medical imaging like X-rays. While experienced radiologists may discern relevant details, general viewers might be confused by prominent colors, such as large red areas, which detract from the subtleties of fractures or other important features. The goal is to enhance resolution in areas of interest rather than draw attention to misleading color representations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what I'm going to talk a little bit about is why jet color map is not useful and what can go wrong with the color map. So for example, what you're seeing at the top at the x-ray is that if a person is a radiologist and they want to take a look at this X-ray. They would basically take a look and say that, okay, there are bones here, they're interesting, they're interesting, but maybe they're more interested in the nuances that are happening here around where there might be some fracture in the bone or whatever. If you visualize the colors on this X-ray using jet color map, you're seeing that there is this big red area that might be distracting. So although an experience, radiologist is not going to be distracted by this kind of information. a general person might get distracted by this information. And what we're interested in is actually having more resolution for the areas that are of more interest. So the reason that you see those spikes in the red area so that it draws the attention of a viewer is because of something like this. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: So what I'm going to talk a little bit about is why jet color map is not useful and what can go wrong with the color map. So for example, what you're seeing at the top at the x-ray is that if a person is a radiologist and they want to take a look at this X-ray. They would basically take a look and say that, okay, there are bones here, they're interesting, they're interesting, but maybe they're more interested in the nuances that are happening here around where there might be some fracture in the bone or whatever. If you visualize the colors on this X-ray using jet color map, you're seeing that there is this big red area that might be distracting. So although an experience, radiologist is not going to be distracted by this kind of information. a general person might get distracted by this information. And what we're interested in is actually having more resolution for the areas that are of more interest. So the reason that you see those spikes in the red area so that it draws the attention of a viewer is because of something like this.

children.title: Perceptually Uniform Color Maps in Visualization
children.summary: The discussion highlights the issues with using the jet color map in data visualization, particularly its non-uniformity in lightness which can mislead viewers. A perceptually uniform color map, like the Veritas Color Map developed by the Berkeley Institute of Data Science, maintains a consistent lightness trend, improving the effectiveness of data communication and being friendly for color-blind users. The importance of ensuring uniform distribution in color maps for accurate representation of data is emphasized.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So you would see that, OK, if I have set of intensities that I want to visualize and I visualize it using gray scale, there are some of these bright areas that are good. But if I go ahead and visualize it using jet color map, you're seeing that there are these red areas that are unnecessarily red. And then it again draws the attention of a viewer that might not be experts in evaluating what's happening in this state. The concept that we're trying to use here and figure out why that's problematic is. The concept of a color map being perceptually uniform. What that means is that if we take a look at the lightness of a color map and take a look at the range of colors that we have in that color map. And we take a look at the delta in the lightness in that color map. We also we want to make sure that the distribution is kind of uniform in a sense that, okay, if I'm going from, for example, lightness 0.1 to lightness, for example, 0.2, and I see this as specific delta. And if I go, for example, from 0.4 or 0.5 to 0.6 in this area, I should see exactly the same trend and the same like increase or decrease in the lightness. So in this case, not only that it's not increasing, but that the lightness also decreases as the color changes. So that's why you're seeing that with the jets color map, we're seeing that the lightness bounces all over the place and it's not a linear trend. But with the, with the, With our very best color map what's happening is that this loop is constant so it's a Conceptually uniform color map that we can use so not only that it's like Red green blind kind of friendly It's also going to communicate results more effectively or communicate color more effectively to the viewer So this Veritas Color Map is created by a group at Berkeley. So this is actually from Berkeley Institute of Data Science, who created this Color Map. And now the default Color Map that you're using in C-Born and Math, let lib1 from, I think, Jet Color Map to Um. We're at this column map that we have right here. So the default initially was jet column map. The other place that we can use color is when we are trying to include categories. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: So you would see that, OK, if I have set of intensities that I want to visualize and I visualize it using gray scale, there are some of these bright areas that are good. But if I go ahead and visualize it using jet color map, you're seeing that there are these red areas that are unnecessarily red. And then it again draws the attention of a viewer that might not be experts in evaluating what's happening in this state. The concept that we're trying to use here and figure out why that's problematic is. The concept of a color map being perceptually uniform. What that means is that if we take a look at the lightness of a color map and take a look at the range of colors that we have in that color map. And we take a look at the delta in the lightness in that color map. We also we want to make sure that the distribution is kind of uniform in a sense that, okay, if I'm going from, for example, lightness 0.1 to lightness, for example, 0.2, and I see this as specific delta. And if I go, for example, from 0.4 or 0.5 to 0.6 in this area, I should see exactly the same trend and the same like increase or decrease in the lightness. So in this case, not only that it's not increasing, but that the lightness also decreases as the color changes. So that's why you're seeing that with the jets color map, we're seeing that the lightness bounces all over the place and it's not a linear trend. But with the, with the, With our very best color map what's happening is that this loop is constant so it's a Conceptually uniform color map that we can use so not only that it's like Red green blind kind of friendly It's also going to communicate results more effectively or communicate color more effectively to the viewer So this Veritas Color Map is created by a group at Berkeley. So this is actually from Berkeley Institute of Data Science, who created this Color Map. And now the default Color Map that you're using in C-Born and Math, let lib1 from, I think, Jet Color Map to Um. We're at this column map that we have right here. So the default initially was jet column map. The other place that we can use color is when we are trying to include categories.

children.title: Using Color in Data Visualization
children.summary: Color can be used to distinguish between qualitative and quantitative variables in data visualization. For qualitative data, a qualitative color scheme should be chosen to differentiate categories, while for quantitative data, a sequential or divergent color scheme can visualize magnitude. Sequential color maps transition from light to dark colors, while divergent color maps highlight variations around a midpoint.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So if I, for example, here, want to go ahead and plot different kinds of flowers based on certain attributes, I could say that go ahead and create the KDE plots for different types of colors separately and use the color channel in order to distinguish between categories. So the most effective place to use the color in the case that there is a combination of qualitative variables and quantitative variable is to visualize categories using the color in terms of visualizing qualitative variables. How can we use color more efficiently is that we can have different kinds of color schemes or color maps. So for example, in the left hand side, I'm using a color map that is sequential. So it starts from some of these high numbers from some of the high numbers to be colored as white. And then it goes all the way to this dark, this dark color that we have. And that's how we can visualize these numbers in a sequential way. There's also the type of coloring scheme that is called divergent coloring scheme. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: So if I, for example, here, want to go ahead and plot different kinds of flowers based on certain attributes, I could say that go ahead and create the KDE plots for different types of colors separately and use the color channel in order to distinguish between categories. So the most effective place to use the color in the case that there is a combination of qualitative variables and quantitative variable is to visualize categories using the color in terms of visualizing qualitative variables. How can we use color more efficiently is that we can have different kinds of color schemes or color maps. So for example, in the left hand side, I'm using a color map that is sequential. So it starts from some of these high numbers from some of the high numbers to be colored as white. And then it goes all the way to this dark, this dark color that we have. And that's how we can visualize these numbers in a sequential way. There's also the type of coloring scheme that is called divergent coloring scheme.

children.title: Divergent and Qualitative Colormaps in Visualization
children.summary: Divergent colormaps are used to represent opposing views in data visualization, allowing for distinct color representation without sequential encoding. Examples include color maps like plasma, inferno, and magma, which are conceptually uniform and avoid issues present in the jet colormap. Qualitative colormaps can be selected for categorical data to ensure no implied sequence among categories.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So if you want to communicate to opposite kind of views And two different specific categories that you don't want to make them sequential to incode any order, you could actually use divergent column map. So here's the column map. I think encoding different national governor suings in different states. So that's why you see some of the states be blue and some of the states be red. And it really doesn't make sense to put them on a purple sequential color scale. They should be separate colors communicating different views, specifically using the plot. Thankfully, MacPlove has a bunch of different color maps that you can directly use. You just specify the color map that you want to use. And now you know what to use at what point. So for example, here, there is the very best color map. You can take a look at the sequence. You can take a look at the range values. There's plasma, inferno, magma, and each one of them have their own kind of characteristics. And the important part is that all of them are conceptually uniform. So they don't have any issue that we had with the jet color map. We could also have color maps that are qualitative, meaning that if you have different categories that you don't want them to be sequenced in a certain way, you can pick a set of colors to represent different categories. leaf_chunk.audio: So if you want to communicate to opposite kind of views And two different specific categories that you don't want to make them sequential to incode any order, you could actually use divergent column map. So here's the column map. I think encoding different national governor suings in different states. So that's why you see some of the states be blue and some of the states be red. And it really doesn't make sense to put them on a purple sequential color scale. They should be separate colors communicating different views, specifically using the plot. Thankfully, MacPlove has a bunch of different color maps that you can directly use. You just specify the color map that you want to use. And now you know what to use at what point. So for example, here, there is the very best color map. You can take a look at the sequence. You can take a look at the range values. There's plasma, inferno, magma, and each one of them have their own kind of characteristics. And the important part is that all of them are conceptually uniform. So they don't have any issue that we had with the jet color map. We could also have color maps that are qualitative, meaning that if you have different categories that you don't want them to be sequenced in a certain way, you can pick a set of colors to represent different categories.

children.title: Color Maps: Qualitative, Sequential, and Divergent
children.summary: Qualitative color maps are used for categorical data, while sequential color maps transition from light to intense colors to represent quantitative values. Divergent color maps start and end with different colors, centering on white to indicate neutrality. Marking is highlighted as an important characteristic in data visualization.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And that's why they're called qualitative color maps. We can also have sequential color maps starting from generally in the white areas going into like intense specific colors that you can use in order to include a sequential quantitative value. We can also have divergent color maps that are starting from a certain color. They end in another color and at the center they have their all white. At the center that there's no bias into any of those divergent categories, you would get the white color when you're representing all of those categories. Marking is also an important characteristic. What to use for markings? Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

leaf_chunk.audio: And that's why they're called qualitative color maps. We can also have sequential color maps starting from generally in the white areas going into like intense specific colors that you can use in order to include a sequential quantitative value. We can also have divergent color maps that are starting from a certain color. They end in another color and at the center they have their all white. At the center that there's no bias into any of those divergent categories, you would get the white color when you're representing all of those categories. Marking is also an important characteristic.
