children.title: Human Perception and Visualization Limitations
children.summary: An exercise demonstrated that humans struggle to accurately perceive area compared to length, as shown by participants estimating a bar seven times longer and a circle seven times larger. Pie charts are ineffective for interpreting angles, leading to inaccurate area comparisons; scatter plots are recommended for clarity. WordClouds can obscure category differences, while bar charts should avoid baseline manipulation. Line charts are preferred for comparing income differences by degree, emphasizing the importance of context, clear labeling, and effective visual channel use in visualizations.

children.title: Human Perception of Length and Area
children.summary: An exercise explored human perception of length and area by comparing the lengths of bars and sizes of circles. Participants estimated a bar that was seven times longer and a circle that was seven times bigger, illustrating the difficulty in accurately perceiving area compared to length.

children.title: Human Perception of Length and Area
children.summary: An exercise was conducted to explore human perception of length and area by comparing the lengths of bars and sizes of circles. Participants estimated the longer bar and larger circle, with the correct answers being seven times longer and seven times bigger, respectively. The difficulty in accurately perceiving area compared to length was highlighted, demonstrating the challenges in interpreting different types of markings.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Remember that I had those lines that I was putting for the rock flood than the night-channage that into those circles. But the marking also has an additional information that how do you want to represent any specific variable? So we're going to do a quick exercise. We still have five minutes, so we can do this fun exercise to figure out how your perception actually works, and how these markings are actually different from each other. So the question I have for you is how much longer is the long bar? So you can actually go measured by your hand. and respond to this slide apple. 4 times longer, 5 times, 6 times, we can do this. But that's literally human perception. Raise your hand if you think four times longer. 5 6 times longer. Good, 7. Okay, eight. Do we have eight? Okay, I, the correct answer is seven. The longer bar is seven times longer than the smaller bar, and this is kind of the proof. But things are going to get more interesting because the idea was for me to show you that all of these markings that we have here starting from lengths going to the, to the slope and angle going to the area are going to be harder for people or a human viewer to perceive. And we're going to do that exercise with an actual other example. How much bigger is the big circle? Let's see. So you could actually do this and then go around the bigger circle. 4 times bigger. Yeah, five. Less 5-6. 6 times bigger 7 Hey! Okay, let's see. It is actually still seven times bigger. You see you're all confused because area measuring area or including information using area is really hard for human to perceive. Video: 1: V

leaf_chunk.audio: Remember that I had those lines that I was putting for the rock flood than the night-channage that into those circles. But the marking also has an additional information that how do you want to represent any specific variable? So we're going to do a quick exercise. We still have five minutes, so we can do this fun exercise to figure out how your perception actually works, and how these markings are actually different from each other. So the question I have for you is how much longer is the long bar? So you can actually go measured by your hand. and respond to this slide apple. 4 times longer, 5 times, 6 times, we can do this. But that's literally human perception. Raise your hand if you think four times longer. 5 6 times longer. Good, 7. Okay, eight. Do we have eight? Okay, I, the correct answer is seven. The longer bar is seven times longer than the smaller bar, and this is kind of the proof. But things are going to get more interesting because the idea was for me to show you that all of these markings that we have here starting from lengths going to the, to the slope and angle going to the area are going to be harder for people or a human viewer to perceive. And we're going to do that exercise with an actual other example. How much bigger is the big circle? Let's see. So you could actually do this and then go around the bigger circle. 4 times bigger. Yeah, five. Less 5-6. 6 times bigger 7 Hey! Okay, let's see. It is actually still seven times bigger. You see you're all confused because area measuring area or including information using area is really hard for human to perceive.

children.title: Limitations of Pie Charts and Area Visualization
children.summary: Pie charts are ineffective due to difficulties in interpreting angles, as demonstrated by the area comparison of circles. Area visualizations often lead to inaccurate size comparisons, with scatter plots recommended for clarity. WordClouds can obscure category differences, and bar charts should avoid baseline juggling. Line charts are preferred for comparing income differences by degree, with an emphasis on feature engineering and effective juxtaposition. Context is vital in visualizations for personal exploration and reporting, requiring clear labeling and effective use of visual channels.

children.title: Pie Charts: Limitations of Area and Angle
children.summary: The area of the larger circle is seven times that of the smaller circle, illustrating the difficulty in accurately interpreting pie charts. Lengths are easier to distinguish than angles, making pie charts less effective for data visualization. The segment emphasizes avoiding pie charts due to inaccuracies in visual angle judgments.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And in fact, this is kind of the validation that the bigger circle is seven times bigger than the smaller circle. So if you take a look at all of these visualizations, you could basically see why the order of those channels are kind of shown in that slide with the same order that we had. It's really easy to measure or take a look at the differences in length channel in the left hand side versus what you're seeing in terms of the area using pie chart, not areas, sorry, the angle using the pie chart. So that's why pie charts are generally less interesting for us to use. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza

children.title: Challenges of Area Visualization in Data
children.summary: Visual area judgments are often inaccurate, making it difficult to compare sizes effectively, as demonstrated with South Africa's GDP compared to Algeria's. Scatter plots are recommended over area charts for clearer distinctions, as they utilize length dimensions rather than area.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Same thing with the area. So remember that we were not so good with the area calculation in the circle. It's really hard for us to see that South Africa here is twice the size of L-G area. But if we were basically plotting it using this kind of scatter plot, it would be easier for us to see because we're taking a look at the differences using the length dimension as opposed to the area dimension. So area is really hard. and in the same kind of way because areas hard we are also less interested in. Plotting is stuff using WordCloud. We're using it in the lecture. children.title: Challenges in WordCloud Visualization
children.summary: WordCloud visualizations can obscure differences between categories due to area encoding, making comparisons difficult. It's important to avoid juggling the baseline in bar charts, as this complicates comparisons across categories. Additionally, stacking data can hinder the ability to accurately compare lengths, emphasizing the need for clear and accurate plotting conditions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's fun to use it. But WordCloud visualization is really hard for people to kind of see the differences between the categories because it encodes area. There are some other considerations with how to encode the channel. One important note is that we don't want you to juggle the baseline. So the juggle baseline means that for all of these bars, the blue region is kind of of the similar lengths or the similar area also. But we are not able to compare them because we're juggling the baseline using all of the additional kind of categories that we're plotting. the same thing with the X dimension, it doesn't matter if you juggle the baseline, it's really hard to to compare categories with each other. We also don't like to stack this stuff on top of each other. So here it's really hard to compare the lengths when there is stack to each other as opposed to just looking at each one of those individual lines plotted separately. So this is also another thing to consider. With the conditioning a lot of time we want to make sure that the conditions that we want to plot are plotted accurately and we're comparing apples to apples. leaf_chunk.audio: It's fun to use it. But WordCloud visualization is really hard for people to kind of see the differences between the categories because it encodes area. There are some other considerations with how to encode the channel. One important note is that we don't want you to juggle the baseline. So the juggle baseline means that for all of these bars, the blue region is kind of of the similar lengths or the similar area also. But we are not able to compare them because we're juggling the baseline using all of the additional kind of categories that we're plotting. the same thing with the X dimension, it doesn't matter if you juggle the baseline, it's really hard to to compare categories with each other. We also don't like to stack this stuff on top of each other. So here it's really hard to compare the lengths when there is stack to each other as opposed to just looking at each one of those individual lines plotted separately. So this is also another thing to consider. With the conditioning a lot of time we want to make sure that the conditions that we want to plot are plotted accurately and we're comparing apples to apples.

children.title: Visualizing Income Differences by Degree
children.summary: To compare income differences between men and women across various degrees, a line chart is more effective than bar charts for visual comparison. Feature engineering is crucial; instead of plotting raw values, variations or engineered metrics should be used. Juxtaposition allows for side-by-side comparisons, while small multiples enable accurate comparisons across different conditions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is an example that if I want to take a look at the differences in incomes for men and women for different degrees that they have, this is not a good way of comparing that condition because you're not going to be able to compare this red bar with this red bar more efficiently. What you can do instead is to have a line chart for each one of them so that you can compare which one of them is bigger than the other one kind of more visually. We could also get more fancier and calculate the ratio. This is why feature engineering is really important that instead of plotting individual values, plot the variation of it or the engineered version of those values that we have. We could also include condition using juxtaposition as the post to super position super position is that you plot multiple things on the same plot. We could also put different conditions side by side is called a small multiples that we're plotting so that for different conditions you're plotting different things side by side so that you can compare them more accurately. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza X

leaf_chunk.audio: So this is an example that if I want to take a look at the differences in incomes for men and women for different degrees that they have, this is not a good way of comparing that condition because you're not going to be able to compare this red bar with this red bar more efficiently. What you can do instead is to have a line chart for each one of them so that you can compare which one of them is bigger than the other one kind of more visually. We could also get more fancier and calculate the ratio. This is why feature engineering is really important that instead of plotting individual values, plot the variation of it or the engineered version of those values that we have. We could also include condition using juxtaposition as the post to super position super position is that you plot multiple things on the same plot. We could also put different conditions side by side is called a small multiples that we're plotting so that for different conditions you're plotting different things side by side so that you can compare them more accurately.

children.title: Importance of Context in Visualization
children.summary: Context is crucial in data visualization, serving two main purposes: personal exploration and reporting. For personal use, visualizations can be simple and informal, while for reporting, it's essential to use all visual channels effectively, ensuring that every plot includes proper labels, titles, and legends.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The last thing that I want to briefly talk about is that context is really important. Again, going back to two reasons that we do visualization, we do visualization for yourself into ETA and we do visualization for reporting. If you do it for yourself, it can be lightweight. It can be just a simple, not so nice map-lovely plot that you're creating. If you're trying to plot something for reporting, then you need to consider using all the channels more efficiently, have every plot that you have X label, Y label, title, nice, also legend on all of the plots and so on and so forth. leaf_chunk.audio: The last thing that I want to briefly talk about is that context is really important. Again, going back to two reasons that we do visualization, we do visualization for yourself into ETA and we do visualization for reporting. If you do it for yourself, it can be lightweight. It can be just a simple, not so nice map-lovely plot that you're creating. If you're trying to plot something for reporting, then you need to consider using all the channels more efficiently, have every plot that you have X label, Y label, title, nice, also legend on all of the plots and so on and so forth.

children.title: Key Takeaways on Visualization Techniques
children.summary: The lecture concluded with a summary of key visualization techniques, emphasizing the ability to create various plots, efficiently plot different variables, and understand the associations between them. It highlighted the importance of utilizing all available information channels on the plot, including effective color scaling. Students were reminded to include informative titles and axis labels in their publication-ready plots.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is the last thing that we'll have on the visualization. Kind of as a takeaway point is that now you know how to create different plots, how to efficiently plot different variables, how to plot association between different variables, and then how to use all the information channels that you have available on the canvas of the plot more efficiently and consider color is scaling someone and so forth. Thank you for coming. We will see you on Thursday. Video: 1: Lec 08 - DS100 Fa24 - Visualiza
